parties: Red Kiwi S.r.l. v Community Plant Variety Office
title: CPVO BoA A013/2020 - Red Queen
text:
Appeal case: A013/2020
DECISION
of the Board of Appeal of 3 November 2021
Concerning the appeal lodged by
Red Kiwi S.r.l.
Via Pietro Nenni, 144 IT – 48025 Riolo Terme, Italy
Representative: Vondst advocaten
Jacob Obrechtstraat 56, NL – 1070 AT Amsterdam, The Netherlands
Represented by Mr T. Overdijk Appellant
Community Plant Variety Office (CPVO)
3, Boulevard Maréchal Foch, CS 10121, F-49101 Angers Cedex 02, France
Represented by Ms O. Lamberti Defendant
Relating to Community Plant Variety Right application 2019/0516
Variety denomination: ‘Red Queen’
Species: Actinidia chinensis Planch.
The Board of Appeal of the Community Plant Variety Office, composed of Mr P. van der Kooij (Chairman), Mr
A. Pohlmann (Rapporteur), and Mr. C. van Ettekoven (Member), gave the following decision:
1. The decision GSE/(20200080571) of 24 September 2020 relating to Community Plant Variety Right
application 2019/0516 is cancelled.
2. The CPVO is ordered to set the application date of Community Plant Variety Right application 2019/0516
to 23 February 2019 and to correct the Register of Applications for Community Plant Variety Rights
accordingly.
3. The CPVO bears the costs of the appeal proceedings.
4. The appeal fee is refunded.

I. SUMMARY OF FACTS
1. On 23 February 2019, Red Kiwi S.r.l. filed an application for a Community Plant Variety Right
(hereinafter ‘CPVR’) for the Actinidia chinensis Planch. variety with denomination ‘Red Queen’. The
application was registered at the CPVO under file No 2019/0516. The Technical Questionnaire
(hereinafter ‘TQ’) for Trees/Shrubs/Woody climbers (Ornamental) TQ-G05 was submitted as part of
the application documents.
2. On 26 February 2019, the applicant paid the relevant application fee.
3. On 27 February 2019, the CPVO sent a communication to the applicant including the following two
points (hereinafter ‘Point 1 of the communication’ and ‘Point 2 of the communication’):
1) No date of application can be acknowledged:
Your application is not complete and does not comply with the following conditions (Article 50(1) of
Regulation No 2100/94 and Article 18 of the Implementing Rules).
 The description of the candidate variety is insufficient. Please complete the
enclosed specific technical questionnaire for Kiwifruit (CPVO/TQ-098/2) and send
it back as soon as possible.
The date of receipt of the information requested above shall be considered as the date of application. If
the information requested above is not received by the Office within one month from the date of issue
of this receipt, the application will be deemed as not having been filed.
2) The application is incomplete:
 Please provide a deed of assignment or a valid declaration whereby all the
intellectual property rights of the candidate variety have been transferred from the
original breeder (the employee) to his company (the applicant and future title
holder of the Community plant variety right). Kindly find attached an explanatory
note on assignments.
4. On 11 March 2019, the applicant provided the TQ for kiwifruit varieties duly filled in. The CPVO
granted the application date of 11 March 2019, corresponding to the said communication pursuant
to Article 18(1) of Commission Regulation (EC) No 874/2009 (hereinafter ‘PR’ (Proceedings
Regulation)).
5. On 10 December 2019, the applicant asked to reconsider the application date the CPVO attributed.
6. By reply of 10 January 2020, the CPVO explained that the technical description of the variety shall
enable a proper organization of the technical examination of the variety, in particular, it shall contain
information on the grouping characteristics of the candidate variety. The CPVO added that, since
the applicant used a different TQ form for its application, the following technical information for the
proper organization of the technical examination was missing:
(a) Fruit: weight;
(b) Fruit: shape;
(c) Fruit: stylar end;
(d) Fruit: hairiness of skin;
(e) Fruit: colour of outer pericarp.
2 / 12

7. By letter of 10 June 2020, the applicant requested the CPVO to review and reconsider the application
date and to amend the CPVO database in order to reflect the correct date of application, i.e. 23
February 2019, which is the date on which the CPVO duly received the application.
8. By decision of 24 September 2020 (hereinafter also ‘the contested decision’), the CPVO refused the
applicant’s request to reconsider the application date granted by the CPVO (Decision No.
GSE/(20200080571)). These are the main arguments of the decision:
- The Basic Regulation establishes that an application date can only be granted when the CPVO
is in receipt of valid technical information. The applicable Regulations contain a list of essential
elements that have to be complied with together with an application for CPVR protection in
order for it to be granted an application date pursuant to Article 51 of Regulation No 2100/94
(hereinafter BR (Basic Regulation)). When the applicant does not provide certain information
in his application, pursuant to Article 18 PR, the Office will invite him to remedy such deficiency
but, if it refers to one of the elements of Article 50(1) BR, the application date will be the one
of the remedying action.
- Once the CPVO established that the application sent by the appellant contained a deficiency, it
sent the relevant communication, inviting the appellant to remedy the deficiency of the
application pursuant to Article 18 PR. The CPVO communication included the technical
questionnaire TQ-098/2 in order to remedy the deficiency. According to the same rules, the
CPVO communicated to the applicant that the date of receipt of the requested information
would be considered to be the application date.
- In the communication of 27 February 2019, the CPVO explicitly stated the following: ‘The
description of the candidate variety is insufficient. Please complete the enclosed specific
technical questionnaire for Kiwifruit (CPVO/TQ-098/2) and send it back as soon as possible’.
Therefore, the applicant was duly informed of the fact that his application was not complete
due to the insufficiency of the technical information furnished and was provided with a copy of
the TQ to be filled in in order to remedy the situation. The fact that, once provided with the
specific TQ form, the appellant provided the appropriate information demonstrates that the
instructions of the Office were sufficient and appropriate.
- In its communication of 27 February 2019, the CPVO added that ‘the date of receipt of the
information requested above shall be considered as the date of application’, thus clarifying the
consequences of the deficiency affecting the granting of an application date.
- It is true that the use of the TQ-098/2 is not imperative. However, it must be noted that if other
TQs are used, all the information relevant for the crop (specified in the specific TQ for the crop)
must be provided in whichever part of another TQ than the specific one. In other terms, the
CPVO needs to be in receipt of the complete information necessary to organize the DUS trial,
regardless of the form used. Nevertheless, the appellant initially did not provide the complete
set of information needed.
- The TQ initially filled in and provided admittedly included several fruit characteristics, as claimed
by the applicant. However, it did not include all the characteristics needed for the organization
of the DUS trial for Actinidia chinensis Planch. varieties.
- The testing of the candidate variety, including the grouping, is performed by the Examination
Office on the basis of the essential elements to be provided by the applicant. In particular, the
CPVO has to be in receipt of information on the states of expression of the candidate variety in
respect of the so called "grouping characteristics"; which are listed in section 5 of the TQ. Said
information is essential to the Examination Office in order to choose which varieties of common
knowledge are to be excluded and which are to be included as reference varieties in the DUS
testing, i.e. to select varieties for the growing trials, and it cannot be inferred from any other
element besides the information provided by the applicant.
9. On 28 September 2020, the contested decision was notified to the applicant.
3 / 12

10. On 24 November 2020, the appellant lodged an appeal against the CPVO decision of
24 September 2020. The grounds of appeal were received by the CPVO on 28 January 2021.
11. On 10 February 2021, an interlocutory revision of the appeal was performed by the Rectification
Committee of the CPVO, which concluded to maintain the appealed decision. In the grounds of the
decision, the Rectification Committee pointed out that the filing date of 23 February 2019 could also
not be granted because a valid deed of assignment had been submitted by the appellant only on
11 March 2019 (Article 50(1)(d) BR). In its communication of 27 February 2019, the CPVO had
already requested the appellant to provide a deed of assignment.
12. The oral hearing took place on 16 September 2021.
13. The appellant requests the Board of Appeal to
o annul the decision of the CPVO of 24 September 2020, in which the formal amendment
of the application date to 23 February 2019, and its recording in the register was
refused, and order the CPVO to formally change the date of the Application to
23 February 2019, and to amend the CPVO Register to reflect this change;
o award the costs of the appeal proceedings to the appellant.
14. The main arguments of the appellant during the appeal proceedings can be summarized as follows:
- According to Article 50(1)(f) BR, the application for a CPVR must contain a technical description
of the variety. On 23 February 2019, the appellant indeed filed a technical description (TQ-G05)
together with the application form. The use of the form TQ/98/2 for the specific DUS Protocol
for Kiwifruit is not imperative. There is no rule that the technical description cannot be provided
with the model questionnaire TQ-G05.
- In the communication of 27 February 2019, the CPVO failed to indicate which information of
the technical description was exactly missing and which information was deemed essential for
the organization of the DUS-test. Said communication addresses only the version of the form
used and does not offer any explanation concerning the technical information that is missing.
The CPVO should have specified the deficiency and it should not have confined itself to stating
that the information showed a deficiency.
- If, for example, the fruit weight was a relevant piece of information for the DUS test, the CPVO
should have simply stated that it needed the fruit weight and the client could have
supplemented the fruit weight forthwith instead of going through the process of filling out a
new form which was as such not imperative or essential.
- The only deficiency that was mentioned by the CPVO (‘Please complete the enclosed specific
technical questionnaire’) was actually not a deficiency, because the use of a specific form or
questionnaire was not imperative.
- It is unclear whether the data indicated by the CPVO communication of 10 January 2020 as
missing were relevant for the organization of the testing. It therefore cannot be said that the
technical description was 'insufficient' for the purposes of Article 50 BR and for the purpose of
organizing the DUS-testing.
- Of the five characteristics mentioned by the CPVO in its communication of 10 January 2020,
only one (‘fruit weight’) is marked as mandatory in TQ/98/2, and only in the online version of
the TQ (Annex 3). The offline version of the TQ does not even contain any element ‘fruit weight’
(Annex 4).
- If the online version of the TQ/98/2 marks only certain questions as mandatory, the appellant
can reasonably infer from this that the characteristics which are not marked mandatory are not
considered essential for the purpose of the technical description.
4 / 12

- Consequently, the application and the TQ filed by the appellant on 23 February 2019, were fully
compliant with the requirements of Article 50 BR and applicable implementing rules. The
rationale for the provisions with relevance for determining the date of application is that on the
basis of these provisions and the documents submitted there is a sufficient guarantee as
regards the identity of the appellant and the candidate variety in order to justify that a right of
priority may be deemed to apply as from the date on which a valid application was received by
the CPVO. If additional information would be necessary for the organization of the DUS-test
there is no good reason why this should be an impediment to the CPVO for making a
determination concerning the date of application. It cannot be argued in this connection that if
the CPVO takes the view that it needs additional information for being able to organize the
DUS-test, this need for additional information could be of any concern to third parties.
- Reference is also made to Article 11 UPOV 1991 Act, which sets forth that any breeder who has
duly filed an application for the protection of a variety in one of the Contracting Parties is
entitled to a right of priority in regard to subsequent applications for the same variety in other
Contracting States. In the Explanatory Notes on the Right of Priority under the UPOV
Convention (UPOV/EXN/PRI/1, dated October 22, 2009) reference is made to various aspects
which could obviously be relevant in view of interests of third parties, such as Novelty of the
variety, the determination of Distinctness and the relation with other Variety Denominations.
The organization of the DUS-test by the CPVO which received the first application is not
mentioned as a relevant aspect in this connection.
- Finally, Article 19(3) PR sets forth that during the examination of the application, at any time
after the filing of the application, the CPVO may call for any (additional) information and
documentation which may be necessary for the conduct of the technical examination. This
option provides for yet another reason why the organization of the DUS-test, which is essentially
an internal process of the CPVO, should not be regarded as an aspect which could be taken
into account for the purpose of determining the validity of an application under Article 50 BR.
15. The CPVO requests the Board of Appeal to
o dismiss the appeal;
o order that the appellant bears the costs of the appeal proceedings.
16. The main arguments of the CPVO during the appeal proceedings can be summarized as follows:
- As regards the technical description, Article 50(1)(f) BR combined with Article 51 BR requires a
technical description to be provided, which is taken to mean a complete technical description,
to secure the application date. The CPVO provides a technical questionnaire to all applicants
concerning Kiwifruit applications. That questionnaire must be completely filled out.
- Technical questionnaires are an essential tool for successful examinations, see document
TGP/7/8 concerning the development of test guidelines provided by UPOV.
- Pursuant to Article 16 PR, the CPVO must make available a technical questionnaire for the
species concerned and the applicant must fill it in, sign it, and return it. The format and the
font of the questionnaire are not an absolute requirement, however, complete information is of
the essence. In the case at hand, the information provided by the applicant on
23 February 2019 was not complete: The general questionnaire for climbing ornamental
varieties lacked some characteristics. That additional information was submitted by the
applicant only on 11 March 2019.
- The CPVO provides detailed notes for applicants and detailed online explanations on filing
applications. If the applicant cannot find the technical questionnaire the CPVO can be contacted.
Applicants must avail themselves of this services when filing the application. The online system
also eases the selection of the technical questionnaire.
- The appellant decided in an arbitrary way not to use the correct questionnaire, with the effect
of providing insufficient information. The burden is on the appellant to provide all the necessary
application elements. Some degree of diligence is expected in that regard.
5 / 12

- It is true that the lacking information concerning the technical description was not identified in
Point 1 of the communication dated 27 February 2019. In cases like these, for reasons of
procedural economy, the proper questionnaire is sent to the applicant with the request to fill it
in. The reason for this procedure is to make the application receipt process more efficient and
to treat all applicants equally. The proper questionnaire affords clarity and exhaustiveness in
gathering the necessary information to all involved. The effectiveness of that procedure is
confirmed by the fact that the appellant complied with the request and returned the proper
questionnaire to the CPVO.
- Article 19 PR in combination with Article 50(2) BR only applies if a complete technical
questionnaire has been provided by the applicant. If no complete technical questionnaire is
submitted, Article 50(1) BR applies. The purpose of Article 50(1)(f) BR is to organize a robust
DUS trial and to grant robust intellectual property rights. An application date cannot be granted
on the basis of an incomplete description of the variety.
- Concerning the argument that only the characteristic ‘Fruit: weight’ was indicated as being
mandatory in the online form TQ-098/2, a new technical questionnaire is now available for
kiwifruits, which includes additional mandatory fields concerning the remaining four missing
characteristics mentioned in the communication of 10 January 2020. Moreover, the concept of
‘mandatory field’ is only an IT concept. It is essential that the applicant at least attempts to fill
in all questions contained in the questionnaire.
- As regards the assignment documents, the legal basis for Point 2 of the communication of
27 February 2019 was Article 50(1)(d) BR in conjunction with Article 11 BR. It is true that Point
2 of the communication did not indicate that the failure to submit the assignment documents
would lead to a postponement of the filing date. However, when performing the formal
examination, the CPVO flags irregularities on entitlement based on a preliminary assessment of
what seems to be missing or incomplete. At that stage, the CPVO does not know what the
entitlement situation is.
- The requested deed of assignment was provided by the appellant on 11 March 2019. This
shows that the deficiency letter of 27 February 2019 was clear to the appellant.
II. REASONS
A. ON THE ADMISSIBILITY OF THE APPEAL
17. According to Article 67(1) BR, an appeal shall lie from decisions of the Office which have been taken,
inter alia, pursuant to Article 61 BR as well as on decisions related to the entering of information in
the Register pursuant to Article 87 BR. The contested decision is a decision pursuant to Article
61(1)(a) BR because the Office refused the application No. 2019/0516 insofar as it did not grant
the original filing date of 23 February 2019. Moreover, the letter also relates to the entering of
information in the Register under Article 87 BR, namely information relating to the date of
application.
18. The appellant was entitled to file an appeal since the letter of 24 September 2020 was addressed
to him (Article 68 BR).
19. Finally, the appeal complies with the form and time limits foreseen in Article 69 BR.
20. The appeal is admissible.
6 / 12

B. ON THE SUBSTANCE
Preliminary remarks
21. The contested decision of 24 September 2020 rejected to reconsider the filing date exclusively on
the ground that the technical description filed together with the application on 23 February 2019
had been incomplete. The contested decision has to be assessed together with Point 1 of the CPVO
communication of 27 February 2019 (see above at para. 3) and the CPVO communication of
10 January 2020. The Board of Appeal will therefore first address the issue of the technical
description.
22. On the other hand, the contested decision was not based on the ground that no deed of assignment
had been submitted by the appellant on 23 February 2019. However, since this argument was raised
by the CPVO in the appeal proceedings, referring to Point 2 of the CPVO communication of
27 February 2019 (see above at para. 3), the Board of Appeal deems it appropriate to deal with this
issue separately.
The technical description
23. Pursuant to Article 50(1)(f) BR, the application for a CPVR must contain a technical description of
the variety.
24. According to Article 51 BR, the date of application for a CPVR shall be the date on which a valid
application was received by the Office or by a sub-office or national agency, provided it complies
with Article 50(1) BR and subject to payment of the application fee.
25. If the CPVO finds that the application does not comply with the conditions laid down in Article 50(1)
BR, it shall notify to the applicant the deficiencies it has found, stating that only such date as
sufficient information remedying those deficiencies is received shall be treated as the date of
application for the purposes of Article 51 BR (Article 18(1) PR).
26. It follows from the aforementioned provisions that non-compliance with Article 50(1)(f) BR may lead
to a postponement of the filing date.
27. On the other hand, if the application, although complying with the conditions referred to in Article
51 BR, does not comply with other conditions laid down in Article 50 BR, the CPVO shall give the
applicant an opportunity to correct any deficiencies that may have been identified (Article 53(2)
BR). ‘Other conditions laid down in Article 50 BR’ are the conditions referred to in Article 50(2) BR.
28. Pursuant to Article 50(2) BR, details of the conditions referred to Article 50(1) BR, including the
provision of further information, may be laid down in the implementing rules. Article 19 PR deals
with the ‘conditions referred to in Article 50(2) BR’. Further details to be provided by the applicant
under Article 50(2) are, in particular, ‘the characteristics of the variety, including the state of
expression for certain characteristics based on the technical questionnaire’ (Article 19(2)(f) PR).
29. Non-compliance with Article 50(2) BR in combination with Article 19(2)(f) PR does not lead to a
postponement of the filing date. Instead, if the CPVO finds that the application does not comply
with Article 19(2)(f) PR, it shall require the applicant to remedy the deficiencies it has found within
7 / 12

such time limit as it may specify. Where those deficiencies are not remedied in good time the CPVO
shall without delay refuse the application (Article 19(1) PR).
30. As a general rule, Article 50(1)(f) BR does not only apply when ‘no technical description’ is submitted
but it may also apply when an ‘incomplete’ technical description is presented, in the sense that the
technical description lacks fundamental or mandatory information in order to grant a filing date.
This requires, however, that a) the missing information is indeed mandatory in order to grant a
filing date and that b) the CPVO explains – either in the communication or in an attachment – which
information is missing and that the filing date will be the date on which the requested information
is received (See Article 18(1) PR).
31. In the case at hand, the contested decision of 24 September 2020 and Point 1 of the CPVO
communication of 27 February 2019 were explicitly based on Article 50(1) BR.
32. The information provided in the filing form and in the accompanying TQ-G05 submitted on
23 February 2019 meets the requirement of a ‘technical description of the plant variety’ under Article
50(1) BR. The documents of 23 February 2019 contain information about the botanical taxon,
partially known crossings, the method of propagation, the plant type, growth habit, its height, the
main colour of the upper side of the leaf, flower type and number of colours, the main colour of the
flower, the diameter and main colour of the fruits, information about similar varieties, additional
information about the size of the fruit and the colour of the fruit flesh, special conditions for growing,
the recommended planting period, and four colour pictures of the plant and the fruits (see points
05.01-07.03 and 07.05 of TQ-G05).
33. The main purpose of providing a ‘technical description’ under Article 50(1) BR is to sufficiently
identify the plant variety in order to grant a filing date for the CPVR. Its primary purpose is not, as
claimed in the contested decision, ‘to complete information necessary to organise the DUS trial’.
That type of information, included in the TQ, can be provided by the appellant under Article 50(2)
BR and Article 19 PR, without losing the original filing date.
34. The fact that the appellant did not use the specific technical questionnaire for Kiwifruit (CPVO/TQ-
098/2) when filing the plant variety application is not decisive. As the Office acknowledged in the
contested decision, the use of a specific technical questionnaire was ‘not imperative’ (Point 11.ii of
the contested decision).
35. In the CPVO communication of 10 January 2020 and in the contested decision, the Office claimed
that the technical description submitted on 23 February 2019 ‘did not contain information on
expression of the candidate variety in the following characteristics: (a) Fruit: weight; (b) Fruit:
shape; (c) Fruit: stylar end; (d) Fruit: hairiness of skin; (e) Fruit: colour of outer pericarp’ (Point
11.iii of the contested decision).
36. In the communication dated 10 January 2020, the CPVO argued that said characteristics were
needed ‘for proper organization of the technical examination’ (Point 3). However, as mentioned
above, the purpose of Article 50(1)(f) BR is not to gather all the necessary information to organise
a DUS trial. The details asked by the Office in the contested decision are ‘characteristics of the
variety, including the state of expression for certain characteristics based on the technical
questionnaire’ under Article 19(2)(f) PR.
37. Furthermore, only one of those characteristics, namely ‘Fruit: weight’ was considered to be a
mandatory requirement in TQ-098/2. All the other characteristics were not mandatory and cannot
therefore lead to a postponement of the filing date. The argument brought forward by the CPVO
8 / 12

that ‘the reasons that a given field is not marked as ‘mandatory’ refers either to IT possibilities
and/or [to the fact] that the question might not be applicable to all variety types covered by a given
TQ’ (Point 11.vii of the contested decision) is not convincing. As mentioned by the CPVO at the oral
hearing, the fields 05.03-05.08 (characteristics of the variety: fruit shape, stylar end, hairiness of
skin, colour of outer pericarp, colour of locules, time of maturity for harvest) are mandatory under
the current TQ-098/3 for kiwifruit (published on 2 July 2021). However, TQ-098/2 did not mark
these fields as mandatory.
38. Moreover, the missing characteristic ‘Fruit: weight’ belongs to the group of quantitative
characteristics. Out of the seven grouping characteristics, only one (‘Fruit: hairiness of skin’) is a
qualitative characteristic. This type of characteristic is considered as the least influenced by the
environment and is therefore ideal for grouping. Three characteristics are so called ‘pseudo-
qualitative’ characteristics that can also be used for grouping but are less reliable depending on the
notes given. Quantitative characteristics like ‘Fruit: weight’ are not very reliable as grouping
characteristics as the differences between the states can be small and the states are on a continuous
scale. In this respect, the characteristic ‘Fruit: weight’ is not different from the other two (non-
mandatory) quantitative characteristics, namely ‘Fruit: shape’ and ‘Time of maturity for harvest’.
Finally, although it is true that the mandatory characteristic ‘Fruit: weight’ was missing in the original
application, the approximate fruit weight can be deduced from the similar variety ‘RS1’ (‘Red Sun
1’) mentioned under point 06.02 of the technical questionnaire filed on 23 February 2019.
Consequently, the fact that one mandatory characteristic was missing in the original questionnaire
is not sufficient to conclude that the information contained in the submitted technical questionnaire
TQ-G05 did not meet the requirement of a ‘technical description of the plant variety’ under Article
50(1)(f) BR.
39. In sum, the information provided by the appellant in the application form and the TQ-G05 was
overall sufficient to confirm that a ‘technical description of the variety’ pursuant to Article 50(1)(f)
BR was submitted on 23 February 2019. Since the contested decision of 24 September 2020 was
based only on Article 50(1)(f) BR (missing technical description), it has to be annulled. For the same
reason, Point 1 of the CPVO communication of 27 February 2019 and the CPVO communication of
10 January 2020 are null and void and do not have any legal effect in the sense that the application
date would be postponed from 23 February 2019 to 11 March 2019.
The assignment documents
40. Under Point 2 of the CPVO communication of 27 February 2019, the Office requested the appellant
to provide a document of assignment showing that all the intellectual property rights of the
candidate variety have been transferred from the original breeder to his company (see above at
para. 3). The CPVO attached an explanatory note on assignments to the communication.
41. Point 1 and Point 2 of the CPVO communication of 27 February 2019 are two independent and
separate administrative acts. The fact that Point 1 of the communication does not have the legal
effect as announced by the CPVO (see above at para. 39) does not influence the validity of Point 2
of said communication.
42. Point 2 of the CPVO communication does not mention any legal basis. In the appeal proceedings,
the CPVO claimed that Point 2 was based on Article 50(1)(d) BR. According to that provision, the
application for a CPVR must contain at least ‘the name of the breeder and an assurance that, to the
best of the applicant’s knowledge, no further persons have been involved in the breeding, or
discovery and development, of the variety; if the applicant is not the breeder, or not the only
breeder, he shall provide the relevant documentary evidence as to how the entitlement to the
Community plant variety right came into his possession’.
9 / 12

43. If Point 2 of the communication of 27 February 2019 was indeed based on Article 50(1)(d) BR, as
claimed by the CPVO in the appeal proceedings, it would be null and void because the CPVO would
have failed to comply with its obligation under Article 18(1) PR to inform the appellant that the
failure to submit a deed of assignment leads to a postponement of the filing date for the purposes
of Article 51 BR.
44. In fact, nothing indicates that the CPVO intended to impose a condition on the appellant, in the
sense that the date of application would depend on the submission of a deed of assignment:
Whereas Point 1 of the communication mentions Article 50(1) BR and Article 18 PR as the legal
basis, no legal basis is included under Point 2 of the communication. Moreover, the appellant was
not informed that the missing deed of assignment might lead to a later filing date. Likewise, the
explanatory note on assignments attached to the communication does not state any of the
aforementioned consequences of not submitting the deed of assignment.
45. When the appellant received the communication of 27 February 2019, he could proceed on the
assumption that the late submission of the deed of assignment would not have any impact on the
filing date. In fact, both the CPVO communication of 10 January 2020 and the contested decision
remain silent as regards the assignment documents.
46. As a general rule, Article 50(1)(d) BR may be a valid legal basis for requesting a deed of assignment
or any other relevant documentary evidence to prove the entitlement of the applicant. This requires
that the CPVO explicitly informs the applicant that the filing date will be the date on which the
requested evidence is received (See Article 18(1) PR).
47. Nevertheless, considering the specific circumstances of the case at hand, the request to provide an
assignment document, as the CPVO did under Point 2 of its communication of 27 February 2019,
could only be rightfully based on Article 50(2) BR in combination with Article 19(2)(b) PR. This was
also the understanding of the applicant and of the CPVO, bearing in mind that the Office never
mentioned in any of its communications or in the contested decision that the missing assignment
document would have any negative consequences for the filing date.
48. The assignment document submitted by the appellant on 11 March 2019 provides missing
information about the appellant’s entitlement to apply for the Community plant variety right. The
assignment document and the explanations given by the appellant complete the information and
evidence provided by the applicant on 23 February 2019: As the breeder, Mr Maurizio dal P.,
explained in his note of 11 March 2019 (‘explanatory note on assignments in Red Kiwi S.r.l.’), the
application was prepared by him and Mr Stefano de N. (the signatory) together and in agreement.
The only reason why Mr Stefano de N. submitted the application form electronically (and therefore
appears as the signatory in the application form) was that ‘he is better at telematic practices, having
more computer skills’. The person behind the application was, however, Mr Maurizio dal P. (see
Point 01.05 of the application form). From his perspective, it therefore made sense to state in the
application form that he was allowed to apply for the variety in the name Red Kiwi S.r.l. because of
his ‘incorporation’ (see Point 05.03 of the application form) in that company (according to the
application form, he is the managing director (‘CEO’) of the appellant) and to submit as ‘proof of
entitlement’ a copy of his identity card with his signature. In his view, he was the breeder and
(indirectly) one of the owners of the appellant, which made further ‘documentary evidence’ about
the entitlement unnecessary.
49. According to Article 19(2)(b) PR, where the applicant is not the breeder, he shall provide the
following information in the application form or in the technical questionnaire: ‘the name and
address of the breeder and his entitlement to apply for the Community plant variety right’. The
10 / 12

assignment document requested on 27 February 2019 is information about the appellant’s
‘entitlement to apply for the Community plant variety right’ (Article 19(2)(b) PR). The request
therefore complies with the wording of that provision.
50. Moreover, using Article 19(2)(b) PR as a legal basis to request the assignment document is also in
line with the purpose of Article 50(1) BR on the one hand and Article 50(2) BR on the other hand.
The primary purpose of Article 50(1) BR is to grant only those applications a filing date which are
sufficiently identifiable. The rationale behind Article 50(2) BR in combination with Article 19 PR is to
gather all the information and documents necessary for the preparation of the DUS trials.
51. It is true that the contents of Article 50(1)(a) - (j) BR and Article 19(2)(a)-(m) PR overlap to a large
extent, which may lead to legal uncertainties as to which of the two provisions is applicable in a
given case. In such a situation, it is necessary to interpret Article 50(1) BR restrictively, bearing in
mind that its main purpose is to gather only the essential minimum information that the application
‘must contain at least’ to safely identify the candidate variety and to grant an application date. One
has to bear in mind that the requirements foreseen under Article 50(1) BR to grant a filing date for
plant varieties go far beyond the requirements for granting a filing date for other European
Intellectual Property rights, for example, European Union trade marks (Article 32 Regulation (EU)
2017/1001 (EUTMR) in combination with Article 31(1) EUTMR), Community designs (Article 38(1)
(EU) No. 6/2002 (CDR) in combination with Article 36(1) CDR) or European Patents (Article 80 of
the Convention on the Grant of European Patents (EPC) in combination with Rule 40 of the
Implementing Regulation to the EPC). A restrictive interpretation of Article 50(1) BR is therefore
appropriate in order to keep the threshold for safeguarding the filing date at an acceptable level.
52. Given the aforesaid, and bearing in mind the specific circumstances of this case, it is justified to
conclude that the correct legal basis for requesting an assignment document on 27 February 2019
was Article 50(2) BR in combination with Article 19(2)(b) PR. The appellant complied with the
request on 11 March 2019. This does not affect, however, the filing date of the application.
53. The fact that Point 1 of the communication of 27 February 2019 included a ‘warning’ about the
possible postponement of the filing date does not change this result. Point 1 of said communication
only deals with the insufficient technical description of the candidate variety, not with the
entitlement of the applicant. As mentioned above, Points 1 and 2 of the communication have to be
seen as two separate administrative acts of the CPVO (see above at para. 41). Moreover, Point 1 of
the communication has never had the legal effect as announced by the CPVO (see above at
para. 39). The consequence of Point 1 (postponement of the filing date) could therefore not have
any impact for Point 2 of the communication.
54. Even if one took the view that Point 2 of the communication of 27 February 2019 was based (or
should have been based) on Article 50(1)(d) BR, this would not justify a postponement of the filing
date from 23 February 2019 to 11 March 2019. If Article 50(1)(d) BR was the correct legal basis
this would lead to the result that Point 2 of said communication would have included an obvious
procedural error because the CPVO would have failed to observe Article 18(1) PR. Since the Office
never rectified, corrected or formally revoked (Article 53(5) PR) any administrative act leading to
the contested decision or the contested decision itself, the appellant’s trust in the validity of Point 2
of the communication must prevail.
Conclusions
55. The information and documents provided by the appellant on 23 February 2019 complied with the
requirement of a ‘technical description of the variety’ pursuant to Article 50(1)(f) BR. The contested
11 / 12

decision of 24 September 2020, which based the postponement of the filing date from
23 February 2019 to 11 March 2019 exclusively on Article 50(1)(f) BR, lacks a valid legal basis and
has to be annulled. Consequently, Point 1 of the CPVO communication of 27 February 2019 does
not have the legal effect announced by the Office, namely a postponement of the filing date.
56. The request to submit assignment documents (Point 2 of the communication of 27 February 2019)
was validly based on Article 50(2) BR in combination with Article 19(2)(b) PR. The explanations and
documents submitted on 11 March 2019 consists of information about the appellant’s ‘entitlement
to apply for the Community plant variety right’ (Article 19(2)(b) PR) and completes the information
and evidence concerning the entitlement provided by the appellant on 23 February 2019. The
submission of the assignment document on 11 March 2019 does not lead to a postponement of the
filing date.
C. ON THE COSTS
57. Pursuant to Article 85(1) BR, the CPVO, as the losing party, must bear the appellant’s costs of the
appeal proceedings.
58. According to Article 83(4) BR, since the appeal is fully successful and since the success of the appeal
was based on facts which were available at the time of the original decision, the appeal fee shall be
refunded.
D. ON LEGAL REMEDIES
59. According to Article 73 BR, an action may be brought before the Court of Justice of the European
Union against this decision within two months of its service.
VAN DER KOOIJ POHLMANN
12 / 12

parties: House Foods Group Inc v Community Plant Variety Office
title: CPVO BoA A018/2021 - SK20
text:
Appeal Case A018/2021
DECISION
of the Board of Appeal of 1 July 2022
Concerning the appeal lodged by
House Foods Group Inc
Osaka, 1-5-7, Mikuriyasakae-machi,
JP - 577-8520 Higashiosaka-shi, JAPAN
Representative:
Meissner Bolte Patentanwälte Rechtsanwälte,
Partnerschaft mbB, Widenmayerstr. 47
DE - 80538 München, GERMANY
Represented by Dr. G. Würtenberger
Appellant
against a decision of
Community Plant Variety Office
3 boulevard Maréchal Foch
CS 10121,
49101 ANGERS CEDEX 2, FRANCE
Represented by Ms. O. Lamberti
Defendant
Relating to Community Plant Variety Right No EU 58241
Variety denomination: SK20
Species: Allium cepa (Cepa Group)
On 1 July 2022, the Board of Appeal of the Community Plant Variety Office, composed of Mr. P. van der Kooij
(Chairman), Mr. R. Brand (Rapporteur) and Ms. V. Melgar (Member), gave the following decision:
1. The appeal is rejected as inadmissible.
2. The Appellant shall bear the costs of the appeal proceedings pursuant to Article 85(1) of
Regulation (EC) No 2100/94.

I SUMMARY OF FACTS
1. The Community Plant Variety Office (hereinafter also CPVO or Office) registered the application for a
Community Plant Variety Right (CPVR) for the onion (Allium cepa) variety ‘SK20’ (Applicant House
Foods Group Inc) on 18 December 2017. The application was given file No 2017/3314.
The examination of the DUS requirements (distinctness, uniformity, stability) was carried out by the
entrusted Examination Office, Naktuinbouw, located in the Netherlands (hereinafter referred to as
‘Examination Office’, EO or ‘Naktuinbouw’). Plants from seeds have been planted in the fields of
Naktuinbouw in spring 2018 (long day trial) and 2019 (short and long day trial). Observations ended
in 2019.
2. The preliminary report was dated 22 January 2018. The Interim Report, dated 17 October 2018, at the
end of the first year of examination, does not mention any information which can affect DUS and
identification of the variety. It indicates that the bulbs harvested from long day crop were with small caliber,
and so the variety was again tested in 2019 under short (sowing in winter) and long (sowing in spring)
day period. This report was sent to the applicant on 23 October 2018.
3. Naktuinbouw produced the positive Final Report of examination on 14 September 2020 together with the
variety description. It concludes that the variety conforms to the DUS requirements. This report was sent
to the applicant on 21 October 2020.
4. Mr. Würtenberger wrote to the CPVO on 21 December 2020: “…With reference to the CPVO's notification
of 21 October 2020 concerning the draft variety description of the candidate variety ‘SK20’, the Applicant
requests that the additional characteristic [(low lachrymatory factor and pyruvic acid)] described in Point
07.02 of the Technical Questionnaire [TQ] is included in the description of the characteristics determining
the scope of protection of the variety. As becomes evident from the document "Additional Description of
Variety" attached to the application, the low lachrymatory factor and pyruvic acid of the candidate variety
is an outstanding property of the same being of high value for the cultivation and in particular use of the
candidate variety….”
5. Mr. Würtenberger sent to the CPVO, on 10 March 2021, the following publications:
- a brief communication: “An onion enzyme that makes the eyes water” - Nature Vol 419, 17 October
2002, p.685 (Brief Communications); GS-201 985;
- a publication in Nature: “Production and characterization of tearless and non-pungent onion” - Scientific
Reports 623779 DOI 10.1038srep23779.pdf;, published on 6 April 2016.
He added that the aim of the breeder was to breed a non-lachrymatory variety and requested to take into
account the additional characteristic.
6. CPVO answered to the applicant on 10 April 2021:
“Following your letters and technical information to support the applicant’s request to add in the variety
description the additional characteristic described in Point 07.02 of the Technical Questionnaire (low
lachrymatory factor and pyruvic acid), we can confirm that the examination of this additional characteristic
has not been considered by the Office and that the outcome of the examination was conclusive on the
basis of the standard characteristics.
As mentioned in previous communications, we need to stick to the CPVO Technical Protocol [TP] and, since
the characteristic the applicant is asking for is not part of it, the examination office had no reason to
observe it during the usual DUS testing.
In addition, we kindly remind you that the Office does not provide a service of testing any characteristic
mentioned by applicants if there is no such need for the purpose of the CPVO decision on the application.
Nevertheless, please be informed that this additional information stands in the file of the variety ‘SK20’.
The CPVO exchanges the technical questionnaires with the EPO. Thus, if someone else claims a patent for
the characteristic in question, there will be a trace that there is prior art in our documents.”
7. On 3 May 2021, the CPVO delivered the certificate on the grant of the CPVR for ‘SK20’ (No: 58241).
2 / 11

8. On 1 July 2021, the Appellant filed a notice of appeal, the receipt of which was acknowledged on 5
July 2021. House Foods Group Inc, represented by Mr. Würtenberger, produced an “Appeal against
latter decision and request that the additional characteristic described in point 7.02 of the technical
questionnaire, namely the low lachrymatory factor and pyruvic acid, is included in the description of
the characteristics determining the scope of the protection of the variety,
in the alternative,
that the technical examination will be reissued to examine the candidate variety with regard to that
characteristic”.
9. The Board of Appeal registry acknowledged receipt, on 5 July 2021, of the appeal contesting:
- the decision to grant CPVR EU 58241,
- and non-compliance of a request filed by the applicant under point 7.2 of the technical questionnaire.
10. On 25 August 2021, Mr. Würtenberger produced the grounds of appeal.
In summary, the following arguments were mentioned. Regulation 2100/94 (hereinafter also Basic
Regulation or BR), in Article 6, does not define which characteristics establishing distinctness have
to be taken into consideration within the examination.
Thus the regulation set up general conditions and allows to take into account the advance in breeding
new varieties. The CPVO is bound to observe in the application of the Basic Regulation the principles
set up by the Convention and the guidelines based on the UPOV Convention. It should therefore be
possible to add characteristics to the ones mentioned in the Technical protocol, if they are useful
provided they meet the conditions set out in the general introduction to DUS examination (TG 1/3,
4.2.3).
The variety ‘SK20’ shows such additional characteristic:
- the amount of lachrymatory factor and pyruvic acid are very low,
- the tear inducing property and pungency are absent or very weak.
The characteristic described is a property of the variety being of high value for the cultivation and use
of the variety and is a remarkable progress in onion breeding.
The additional characteristic was not examined by the EO, nor did the President of the Office consider
whether this characteristic could be a characteristic which was able to make the candidate variety more
distinct.
The additional characteristic might fulfill the general requirements laid down in TG 1/3 (4.2) and Art.
5 (2) BR.
It would have been the duty of the Office to take into consideration the additional characteristic.
Pursuant to point III.6 of the Technical protocol and Art. 83(3) BR an applicant may claim, either in
the TQ or during the test, that a candidate variety has a characteristic which would be useful in
establishing distinctness. If such claim is made, and is supported by reliable technical information, a
special test may be undertaken providing that a technical acceptable test procedure can be drafted.
The applicant has the right that all those characteristics which made the variety distinct are taken into
account. If this is not done, this will be to the (commercial) detriment of the applicant. The scope of
protection of the candidate variety in so far is limited.
The CPVO decision to grant the CPVR does not insert any consideration for the claim of the applicant.
Thus neither the Office, nor the President have complied with their duty to properly examine all the
facts of the application for CPVR with care to exercise the discretion provided by the TP.
11. On 16 September 2021, an interlocutory revision of the appeal was carried out by the Rectification
Committee of the CPVO. It decided not to rectify the contested decision, for the reasons put forward below,
in summary.
On the admissibility of the appeal
The Appellant is in essence asking for an amendment of the variety description in the sense that the
additional characteristic ‘low lachrymatory factor and pyruvic acid’ should be included in the description of
characteristics determining the scope of protection of the variety.
In that connection, the appeal essentially aims at claiming that since the applicant had requested an
additional characteristic, the Office and its President had the duty to assess and add it to the list of
3 / 11

characteristics, since this characteristic has a high value for cultivation and, in particular, for the use of the
variety, and would make the variety itself “even more distinct from other known varieties”. As regards the
subject matter of the appeal, it is clear that the Appellant does not challenge the decision to grant a CPVR
to the variety ‘SK20’ but part of the variety description regarding the list of characteristics.
Under the above circumstances, the Office contends that the Appellant does not have standing to appeal
to the Board of Appeal.
As a general principle of procedural law that the Office shall apply pursuant to Article 81 of the Basic
Regulation the legitimate interest in pursuing an appeal is denied if the requested appeal does not remove
in any case the contested decision. In the current case, the appeal is not even directed against the decision
to grant a CPVR but to a part of the variety description.
Furthermore, the losing party principle is based both on the effects of a decision and on their aptitude to
affect the party concerned which is not the case here as the decision to grant a CPVR for ‘SK20’ has not
been challenged. Nor can the claims put forward in relation to the relevance that the additional
characteristic plays in terms of market use of the variety, be of relevance at the time of assessing whether
a candidate variety satisfies the DUS criteria pursuant to the applicable Technical Protocol. As it is evident
from the CPVR legal framework, value for cultivation and use (VCU) or marketing/competitive
considerations fall outside of the criteria to assess the suitability of a characteristic with a view to grant a
CPVR.
The Board of Appeal, in case No A006/2014 of 29 April 2016 (‘Tang Gold’), declared the appeal inadmissible
in the part in which the Appellant asked an amendment of the variety description with the inclusion of a
number of extra characteristics. The Board of Appeal concluded, “The granting authority has the
discretionary power to decide which characteristics establish the requested clear distinctness between the
candidate variety and the varieties of common knowledge”.
The Committee considers that, mutatis mutandis, the same ratio decidendi of case No A006/2014 as
regards admissibility of the appeal to seek an amendment of part of the variety description of a decision
to grant CPVR equally applies to the case at hand.
For all of the above reasons, the Committee considered that the appeal shall be declared inadmissible
pursuant to Article 49(1) of the Proceedings Regulation (hereinafter also PR).
On the substance
For the sake of completeness, the Committee put forward the following arguments on the substance of
the appeal.
- On the conduct of the technical examination
Under Article 56(2) of the Basic Regulation, the conduct of any technical examinations is to be in
accordance with test guidelines issued by the Administrative Council and any instructions given by the
Office.
The applicable CPVO Technical Protocol is CPVO-TP/046/2 of 1 April 2009. Under section I it is established
that the protocol describes the technical procedures to be followed in order to meet the Council Regulation
2100/94 on Community Plant Variety Rights. The technical procedures have been agreed by the
Administrative Council and are based on general UPOV Document TG 1/3 and UPOV Guideline TG/046/7
dated 09/04/2008 for the conduct of tests for Distinctness, Uniformity and Stability. This protocol applies
to varieties of Allium cepa (Cepa Group).
Under section III (conduct of tests), paragraph 3 (characteristics to be used), it is established that the
“characteristics to be used in DUS tests and preparation of descriptions shall be those referred to in the
Annex 1. All the characteristics shall be used. (..) The Administrative Council empowers the President, in
accordance with Article 23 of Commission Regulation N° 1239/95, to insert additional characteristics and
their expressions in respect of a variety.”
Under section III, paragraph 6 (Special tests), it is established “In accordance with Article 83(3) of Council
Regulation No. 2100/94 an applicant may claim either in the Technical Questionnaire or during the test
that a candidate has a characteristic which would be helpful in establishing distinctness. If such a claim is
made and is supported by reliable technical data, a special test may be undertaken providing that a
technically acceptable test procedure can be devised.” It also says that “Special tests will be undertaken,
4 / 11

with the agreement of the President of CPVO, where distinctness is unlikely to be shown using the
characters listed in the protocol.”
Finally, under section III, paragraph 7.a) (Distinctness), it is established “A candidate variety will be
considered to be distinct if it meets the requirements of Article 7 of Council Regulation No. 2100/94.”
The General Court has stated that the protocols and test guidelines, which are adopted by the
Administrative Council acting on a proposal from the President of the CPVO, “are akin to legal rules which
the CPVO has imposed on itself in order to ensure that technical examinations are properly conducted
under conditions objectively established in advance and that the CPVO is therefore required, in principle,
to comply with them in ongoing procedures before it”.
In view of the above, the Committee concluded that,
First, the technical examination shall be conducted according to the rules established in the technical
protocol as approved by the Administrative Council, to meet the requirements of the Basic Regulation.
Second, the characteristics to be used in DUS tests and preparation of descriptions shall be those referred
to in Annex 1 to the technical protocol.
Third, the applicant may claim in the Technical Questionnaire or during the test that a candidate has a
characteristic, which would be helpful in establishing distinctness. This criterion has a supplementary value
in respect to the compulsory list of characteristics that the Administrative Council of the Office has adopted.
Therefore, any such additional characteristics would be relied upon only in case where it would be helpful
of establishing distinctness and provided that a technically acceptable test procedure can be devised.
Fourth, those special tests will be undertaken only where distinctness is unlikely to be shown relying on
the characteristics listed in the applicable protocol.
Fifth, distinctness shall be assessed under the requirements of Article 7 of the Basic Regulation.
It should also be pointed out that a variety shall be distinguishable from any other plant grouping by the
expression of at least one characteristic (Article 5(2) Basic Regulation). Therefore, in the assessment of
distinctness, the Office shall not verify whether a candidate variety is “even more” distinct from varieties
of common knowledge, but to comply with the applicable legal requirements. In the case at hand, the
variety ‘SK20’ was declared distinct from the varieties of common knowledge. Due to its unique
morphology, in particular in respect of the characteristics foliage waxiness (No. 3), bulb shape (No. 18),
skin colour (No. 23) and adherence of dry skin (No. 21) no similar variety had been identified amongst the
comparison varieties during the examination.
Therefore, the Office was not obliged to request the Examination Office to organize special tests as
requested by the Appellant.
It follows from all the foregoing considerations that in the present case, the technical examination of the
variety ‘SK20’ has been conducted in compliance with the above rules pursuant to the applicable protocol.
No technical matters have been reported by the Examination Office in declaring distinctness on the basis
of the list of characteristics of Annex 1 to the protocol. Therefore, pursuant to the applicable protocol, the
Office did not resort to the said additional characteristic.
- On the power of the President of the CPVO to authorize the use of an additional characteristic
In the first place, pursuant to Article 22(1) of the Proceedings Regulation, the Administrative Council takes
a decision as to the test guidelines upon proposal of the President of the CPVO. Article 22(2) of that
regulation specifies that, in the absence of a decision of the Administrative Council as to test guidelines,
the President of the CPVO may take a provisional decision thereon. Moreover, under Article 23(1) of the
Proceedings Regulation, where the Administrative Council takes a decision on test guidelines, it shall
include a power whereby the President of the CPVO may insert additional characteristics and their
expressions in respect of a variety.
The Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU) stated [See judgment of 8 June 2017 in Schniga GmbH v. CPVO,
case C-625/15P (‘Gala Schnitzer’), ECLI:EU:C:2017:435, paragraphs 54 and 55] that “As the Advocate
General noted, in point 97 of his Opinion, the assessment of the characteristics of a plant variety necessarily
contains a particular uncertainty due to the nature of the object itself to which the technical examination
relates, namely a plant variety, as well as the length of time required to conduct such an examination”.
Furthermore, “In those circumstances, only the flexibility that allows the recognised power of the President
of the CPVO, under Article 23(1) of the implementing regulation, to insert additional characteristics in
respect of a variety is also capable of ensuring the objectivity of the procedure for granting Community
5 / 11

plant variety rights. Thus, an application for a Community plant variety right may not be rejected solely on
the ground that the characteristic of an examined variety, found during the technical examination and
decisive for assessing the distinctiveness of that variety in comparison with other varieties, was not referred
to in either the technical questionnaire completed by the applicant or in the relevant test guidelines and
protocols.”
It follows from the foregoing that the President of the Office exercises a discretionary power when deciding
whether to agree to the use of a proposed additional characteristic and that said flexibility is also capable
of ensuring the objectivity of the procedure for granting Community plant variety rights, as confirmed by
the CJEU in the above-referenced judgment.
Accordingly, the Office and the President did not breach the applicable rules as regards the conduct of the
technical examination of the variety ‘SK20’.
- On the suspensory effect of the appeal
The Appellant has not requested the Office that pursuant to Article 67(2) of the Basic Regulation, the
contested decision not be suspended as a result of the appeal.
In view of the above considerations, the rectification of the contested decision was not granted.
12. On 22 September 2021, the Appellant was informed of the decision of the Rectification Committee.
The case was remitted to the Board of Appeal to examine the appeal, pursuant to Article 71 Council
Regulation (EC) No 2100/94.
13. The hearing in this case before the Board of Appeal was held on 20 April 2022 as virtual hearing by
Video Conference. The statements of the parties to proceedings are summarized below.
13.1 The Appellant recalled principles justifying the grant of an exclusive right to plant breeding, making reference
to the Basic Regulation:
- the purpose of granting a CPVR is to support the investment and the resulting achievement of this
investment in order to give incentives to breeders to invest into further competitive products;
- in order to stimulate the breeding and the development of new varieties, there should be improved
protection compared with the current situation;
- for the purpose of granting a CPVR, an assessment of important characteristics relating to the variety
is necessary; however, these characteristics do not necessarily need to be related to their economic
importance.
For the Appellant, the logic behind these principles, in particular if there are important characteristics of
economic value, is that these characteristics should be taken into consideration in the technical examination
of the candidate variety for granting a CPVR.
13.2 For the Appellant, the arguments of the CPVO, in relation to the non-admissibility of the appeal, disregarded
what should be protected by a CPVR: it is the candidate variety with its specific characteristics to be included
in the description of the characteristics as it determines the scope of the protection. In Article 5(2) BR
defining the object of a CPVR, it is mentioned that any characteristic allowing to differentiate the new
breeding results so as to establish whether a candidate variety is distinct, uniform and stable are to be taken
into account in examination proceedings.
The Appellant complained that in the granting proceedings, neither the CPVO nor the examination office has
mentioned why the outstanding characteristic has not been taken into consideration, although the same had
been mentioned already in the technical questionnaire.
13.3 The Appellant wished to come back to a basic principle contained in the UPOV Convention which is that the
only binding obligations on UPOV members are those contained in the UPOV Convention. General
introduction and technical protocols provide general guidance. The purpose of the relevant technical
guidelines is to determine a minimum of characteristics to be considered in every case and not to exclude
additional characteristics which fulfil the DUS requirements. The characteristics included in the test guidelines
6 / 11

are not necessarily exhaustive and must be extended with additional characteristics if proved to allow to
determine distinctness, stability or uniformity.
As per the admissibility of the appeal, the Appellant recalled that the CPVO relied on the Board of Appeal
decision of appeal case A006/2014 ‘Tang Gold’. The Appellant argued that the CPVO disregarded that the
grant of a protection is the confirmation that the candidate variety fulfils the DUS requirements.
To the Appellant, the CPVO took a false decision because the granted protection does not correspond to the
application and to the candidate variety description. The current variety description limits the scope of
protection as it does not include all the characteristics included in the application of the candidate variety.
The applicant is burdened with a deficiency of the decision and as such, lodging an appeal on the grant of
protection is justified and admissible. The protection turns out its value from the variety description and since
the additional characteristic is not included, the scope of protection is limited and does not acknowledge the
achievements of the applicant.
13.4 With regard to substances, the table of characteristics in the technical protocol CPVO TP/46/2 is not
exhaustive for the reasons mentioned above and should not have prevented the examination office to include
the examination of the additional characteristic declared by the applicant.
In this context, the Appellant referred to two basic principles the CPVO is subject to:
- ‘sound administration’ in accordance with which the CPVO must consider, to a reasonable extent,
factors which are in support or against a protection applied for, which includes really important new
characteristics,
- ‘to give statement of reasons for granting’, which is entirely missing in this case, as no reason has been
given to explain why this additional characteristic was not included when granting the CPVR.
13.5 The CPVO replied with the arguments divided in four main parts: first, the admissibility of this appeal; then
two points of substances which are the assessment of distinctness established on the basis of the technical
protocol and the power of the President to consider additional characteristics; finally, the reply to other
arguments raised in the grounds of appeal.
13.6 Admissibility
The main argument of the CPVO was that this appeal should be considered as inadmissible pursuant to
Article 49(1) PR together with Article 81 BR. Article 49(1) PR states that if the appeal does not comply with
the provisions of the BR, it should be considered inadmissible. Article 81 refers to the basic principle of
procedural law which has to be applied by the CPVO. In this case, the CPVO considered that the Appellant
does not have a locus standi because he does not have an interest in pursuing an appeal. Therefore, the
Appellant does not qualify as a losing party as the right was granted. The interest in pursuing an appeal is
denied if the requested appeal does not remove in any case the contested decision. In this appeal, the
Appellant does not request to annul the grant decision.
The CPVO also referred to recent case-law, in particular case C-281/18 P, in which it is settled that for a
person to have an interest in bringing appeal proceedings, the appeal must be capable, if successful, of
procuring an advantage to that party. In this case, the CPVO did not consider that a successful appeal would
result in giving the advantage that the Appellant seeks.
Contrary to the Appellant, the CPVO considered that the Board of Appeal decision in case ‘Tang Gold’
(A006/2014) applies mutatis mutandis to this case and is relevant, because in said case, the Appellant also
requested additional characteristics to be added to the variety description. In this decision, the Board of
Appeal considered that the granting authority has the discretionary power to decide which characteristics
establish the requested clear distinctness between the candidate variety and the varieties of common
knowledge. Contrary to what the Appellant is claiming, the CPVO had no obligation to take into consideration
any additional characteristic.
7 / 11

When mentioning the innovative value of the additional characteristic, the Appellant makes reference to a
criterion which is external to PVR domain, as the potential market value of the variety (value for cultivation
and use or VCU) is not among the criteria to establish the fulfilment of the DUS requirements, proper of the
marketing authorization system of certain species.
13.7 Substance
13.7.1 The distinctness shall be established on the basis of the technical protocol
The first point in substance is the fact that the distinctness shall be established on the basis of the technical
protocol. Article 55 BR states that where the CPVO has not discovered any impediment to the grant of a
Community plant variety right, the CPVO has to organise a technical examination to assess that the DUS
requirements are satisfied. The concept of distinctness is explained in Articles 5 and 7 BR: the candidate
variety should be clearly distinguishable from any other plant variety of common knowledge by at least one
characteristic. Consequently, the distinctness observed in one characteristic is sufficient to grant a right.
Therefore, the argument of the Appellant that the additional characteristic would make the variety ‘even
more distinct’ cannot be taken into consideration as the distinctness criterion was already satisfied. As to the
conduct of the technical examination (Article 56 of the BR), the CPVO recalled that the test guidelines or
technical protocol used are considered as binding rules (see joint General Court Judgements T-91/14 and
92/14). In this case, the technical protocol applicable was CPVO-TP/046/2 of 2009 in which the organisation
of the technical examination is set, and in particular, in which it is indicated that special tests shall be
undertaken, with the agreement of the President of CPVO, where distinctness is unlikely to be shown using
the characteristics listed in the protocol. In this case, the candidate variety was already morphologically so
distinct from varieties of common knowledge that the examination office could not identify a reference variety
for comparison. So, there was no need to invoke the additional characteristic procedure.
Furthermore, this approach was not only consistent with the law but also with the instructions at UPOV level:
paragraph 4.2.3 of TG 1/3, also mentioned by the Appellant, sets out that the characteristics included in the
individual test guidelines are not necessarily exhaustive and may be expanded with additional characteristics
if that proves to be useful. The CPVO considered that it has discretionary power to establish whether to use
additional characteristics or not, and in this case, given that the variety was fully distinct, there was no need
for an additional characteristic. This was also consistent with what is written in the CPVO internal procedure
on additional characteristics. The distinctness was established on the basis of multiple morphological
characteristics, all listed in Annex 1 of the technical protocol.
13.7.2 The power of the President to consider additional characteristics
The second point in substance is the power of the President to authorise the use of an additional
characteristic. According to the Appellant, the CPVO President had the duty to take a decision on the
additional characteristic once this is suggested in the Technical Questionnaire. The argument of the CPVO is
that there is no duty for the President to invoke the additional characteristic procedure when there is no
need. The CPVO referred to Judgment C-625/15P and Judgement C-534/10P which indeed specify the
discretion of the CPVO given by the scientific nature of the work carried out by the CPVO. The Proceedings
Regulation (Articles 22 and 23) is clear in establishing that a decision of the President on additional
characteristics is an option (not an obligation) to be exercised upon discretion. Moreover, the discretion of
the President cannot be an arbitrary power and has still to comply with the binding rules of the technical
protocol which establishes the use of additional characteristics only when there is an issue in establishing
distinctness.
13.8 The CPVO replied to the other arguments of the Appellant.
The fact that the additional characteristic was indicated in the technical protocol (Section 07.02 indicates
“additional characteristics which may help to distinguish the variety”) does not establish rights for the
applicant as the technical protocol submitted pursuant to Article 50(1)(f) BR has only indicative value. It does
not oblige the CPVO to take additional characteristics into consideration.
8 / 11

Regarding Article 83(3) BR which, according to the Appellant, allows the applicant to request the examination
of additional characteristics, the CPVO disagreed as the purpose of this article is to provide the possibility to
carry out extra tests on the candidate variety by the examination office, with subsequent fees to be borne
by the applicant, when this is needed for the grant of a CPVR, which was not the case for ‘SK20’, as the right
was granted.
The principle of sound administration and ex officio obligation mentioned by the Appellant are not contested
by the CPVO, however the CPVO considered to have taken an informed decision. There were many
exchanges with the examination office in which the technical expert requested clarifications on the reference
variety, and on whether this additional characteristic was relevant. The need to invoke the additional
characteristic was not necessary even if the test was uncomplicated and inexpensive as argued by the
Appellant.
Finally, regarding the argument of the Appellant that the decision does not indicate whether the President
exercised its power concerning additional characteristics nor why the latter were not taken into account, that
the CPVO decision lacked motivation, the CPVO disagreed and stated that it has no need to provide precise
reasoning when granting a plant variety right. When the CPVO grants a right, the legal basis is quoted, the
result of the final report is provided as well as the variety description. Moreover, in this case, the CPVO
communicated with the applicant before granting the title on why the additional characteristic was not taken
into consideration.
13.9 The Appellant highlighted the following.
- The Appellant had the general impression that the CPVO regards the grant of a plant variety right as a purely
administrative act; it neither considers the interest of the breeder of the candidate variety nor the public
interest. The Appellant recalled that the purpose of an exclusive right is to foster breeding activities to create
new varieties that will at the end serve the public (considerations 5 and 11 of the BR).
- Concerning admissibility, the Appellant disagreed on the argument of the CPVO stating that the applicant has
no legal interest in this appeal: the applicant has not only an interest but a right to get a granted right which
has the best extent of protection within the frame given by the BR and important characteristics are an
essential part of this gain. The Appellant agreed that commercial value is not decisive unless the DUS
requirements are not fulfilled. The applicant has a legitimate and legal interest in the appeal because the
applicant did not get what he was asking for, namely a description of the variety representing the candidate
variety. Referring to Art.1.vi. of UPOV Convention, the Appellant recalled that a variety is defined by “the
expression of the characteristics resulting from a given genotype or combination of genotypes, distinguished
from any other plant grouping by the expression of at least one of the said characteristics…”. The full
description of the characteristics of the candidate variety should indeed be part of the granted right. Just
obtaining protection has no value on the market without this specific characteristic.
- The Appellant strongly disagreed on the CPVO argument about the intention of the Appellant to overextend
the scope of protection. The Appellant argued that the IP right has the purpose to give the applicant the best
possible extent of protection for his achievements.
- The technical protocols are of course binding rules but set up as minimum standards in order to guarantee
equal treatment, which do not exclude the possibility of the CPVO and its President to, at least, consider the
request of the applicant to take into consideration an additional characteristic. The CPVO argument, that the
President would have considered additional characteristics only if one of the DUS criteria was not fulfilled,
disregards the purpose of an exclusive right which is to observe characteristics which deserve protection. As
the Schniga case shows, the President has the discretion to carefully decide on additional characteristics to
be taken into account. Protocols are not binding with regard to additional characteristics but binding regarding
a certain level of characteristics which have to be examined. The technical protocols show the flexibility of
the system as they provide the possibility to use other characteristics in case a certain characteristic cannot
be examined. This is necessary to really satisfy the purpose of plant breeding.
- The ex officio examination obliges the CPVO to take additional characteristics into account, or at least to
explain why it has not considered it in the examination of the candidate variety.
9 / 11

13.10 The CPVO highlighted the following.
- The CPVO considered that it respected the binding provisions and general principles. It reminded the
timeline of the application process: once the CPVO received the final report from the examination office,
two months were given to the applicant to comment. The issue of the additional characteristic was raised
by the applicant on the last day for comment and assessed by the CPVO. In fact, this issue had already
been assessed during the examination of the candidate variety by the CPVO which considered not
necessary to invoke this additional characteristic. The CPVO, after an internal consultation, replied to the
applicant in April 2021 why this additional characteristic was not examined.
- The CPVO does not have the obligation to reply to every argument of the Appellant, did assess if this
additional characteristic was useful and concluded that it was not necessary as the candidate variety was
already distinct.
- To tackle the issue of the public interest, the CPVO indeed serves the public at large and applies equal
treatment which means applying the same rules to every applicant. In this case, however, a private interest
and a special treatment are discussed.
- Finally, the CPVO recalled that the characteristics on which the title is granted are morphological and do
not necessarily have a commercial value.
II REASONS
A. On the admissibility of the appeal
14. The appeal is inadmissible pursuant to Article 49(1) of Regulation (EC) No 874/2009 in conjunction with
Article 81 of Regulation (EC) 2100/94.
Pursuant to Article 49(1) PR in conjunction with Article 81 BR the legitimate interest in pursuing an appeal
is denied if the requested appeal does not remove in any case the contested decision, which is the case
here, as the Appellant does not challenge the decision to grant a CPVR to the variety ‘SK20’, but only part
of the variety description regarding the list of characteristics.
The losing party principle is based both on the effects of a decision and on their aptitude to affect the party
concerned which is not the case here as the decision to grant a CPVR for ‘SK20’ has not been challenged.
B. On the substance
15 For the sake of precedence and completeness it is, in spite of the inadmissibility of the appeal, worthwhile
to consider its substance.
15.1 The assessment of distinctness established on the basis of the technical protocol
The distinctness shall be established on the basis of the technical protocol (see point 13.7.2). The candidate
variety should be clearly distinguishable from any other plant variety of common knowledge by at least one
characteristic (Art. 5 and 7 BR), which was the case here. Therefore, the argument of the Appellant that the
