additional characteristic would make the variety ‘(even more) distinct’ cannot be taken into consideration as
the distinctness criterion was already satisfied. There was no need to invoke the additional characteristic
procedure.
Furthermore, this approach was also consistent with the instructions at UPOV level: paragraph 4.2.3 of TG
1/3 sets out that the characteristics included in the individual test guidelines are not necessarily exhaustive
10 / 11

and may be expanded with additional characteristics if that proves to be useful. The CPVO has discretionary
power to establish whether to use additional characteristics or not, and in this case, given that the variety
was fully distinct, there was no need for an additional characteristic. This was also consistent with what is
written in the CPVO internal procedure. The distinctness was established on the basis of multiple
morphological characteristics, all listed in Annex 1 of the technical protocol.
15.2 The power of the President to consider additional characteristics
When an additional characteristic is suggested by an applicant, there is no obligation for the President of the
Office to invoke the procedure laid down in Articles 22 and 23 PR when there is no need to do so, in particular
once the distinctness of the variety has already been established.
This principle is also reflected by Judgments C-625/15P and C-534/10P which specify the discretion of the
CPVO given by the scientific nature of the work carried out by it.
III FINAL CONCLUSION
16. The Board of Appeal does not find decisive arguments in the appeal for repealing the decision of the CPVO
to grant a CPVR to the variety ‘SK20’ given that the technical examination was conducted pursuant to the
Technical Protocol.
These arguments must therefore be rejected in their entirety.
IV ON THE COSTS
17. Since the appeal is rejected as inadmissible the Appellant shall bear the costs of the appeal proceedings
pursuant to Article 85 (1) BR.
V ON LEGAL REMEDIES
18. Pursuant to Article 73 BR, an action may be brought before the Court of Justice of the European Union
against this decision within two months of its service.
Paul VAN DER KOOIJ Richard BRAND
Chairman of the Board of Appeal Rapporteur
11 / 11

parties: Mema S.R.L. Società Agricola v Community Plant Variety Office
title: CPVO BoA A014/2020 - Braeburn 78
text:
APPEAL CASE A014/2020
DECISION
of the Board of Appeal of 23 January 2023
Concerning the appeal lodged by
Mema S.R.L. Società Agricola
Via Chiesa 54
39018 Terlano (BZ)
Italy Appellant
Represented by:
Mr. H. Wichmann and Mr. S. Kirschstein-Friend
Wuesthoff & Wuesthoff
Schweigerstraße 2
81541 München
Germany
Community Plant Variety Office
3 Boulevard Maréchal Foch
CS 10121
49101 Angers Cedex 2
France
Represented by:
Mr. F. Mattina, Ms. M. García-Moncó Fuente,
Ms Á. Martínez López and Ms U. Braun-Mlodecka
Defendant
Relating to Community Plant Variety Right Application No 2009/0954
Variety denomination: ‘Braeburn 78’
Species: Malus domestica Borkh.
On 23 January 2023, the Board of Appeal of the Community Plant Variety Office, composed of Mr. P. van der
Kooij (Chairman), Mr. D. Reheul (Rapporteur) and Mr. H. Koenraad (Member), gave the following decision:
1. The appeal is dismissed.
2. The Appellant shall bear the costs of the appeal proceedings pursuant to Article 85(1)
of Regulation (EC) No 2100/94.

1. SUMMARY OF FACTS
1. On 20 May 2009, the Community Plant Variety Office (CPVO or Office) registered the application for a
Community Plant Variety Right (CPVR) for the apple variety ‘Braeburn 78’ (Malus domestica Borkh). The
application was given file No 2009/0954.
2. The examination of the DUS requirements (distinctness, uniformity, stability) was carried out by the
entrusted Examination Office (EO), GEVES (INRA), with a Testing Centre located in the Angers-Beaucouzé
area (France). DUS tests were conducted in the years 2011, 2012 and 2013, on the basis of the CPVO
Technical Protocol CPVO-TP/14/2 Final of 14th March 2006 (hereinafter: TP/14/2), and the UPOV Guidelines
TG/14/9 of 6 April 2005.
3. On 3 January 2012 the EO delivered the second interim report, announcing that there was no significant
production of fruits in 2011 and requesting the Appellant for 4 extra trees of ‘Braeburn 78’, grafted on
rootstock M9 for direct comparison between ‘Braeburn 78’ and ‘Joburn’ and ‘Toymac’.
4. On 30 January 2013 the EO delivered the third interim report, announcing the first significant fruit production
in 2012 and requesting the Appellant to precise differences between ‘Braeburn 78’ and ‘Royal Braeburn’ and
‘Joburn’.
5. On 9 December 2013 the CPVO sent a message to the Appellant announcing that ‘Braeburn 78’ is not distinct
from ‘Royal Braeburn’ and mentioning the possibility to visit the Testing Centre. The report of this visit on
28 January 2014, signed by both the Appellant and the EO, mentions that apples of ‘Braeburn 78’, compared
to ‘Royal Braeburn’, had a more lively red colouring and a significant (in German: ‘wesentlich) more intense
fruit colouring.
6. On 21 May 2014 the final report of the EO declares ‘Braeburn 78’ as not distinct from ‘Royal Braeburn’ and
‘X9466’1, based on observations on the fruits during 2012 and 2013. The table below is part of the final
report and concerns observations on the fruits during 2012 and 2013.
1
‘X9466’ is not a reference variety but concerned another candidate variety that was included in the DUS test for
‘Braeburn 78’, but for which no plant variety rights were granted.
2

7. During the months June to December 2014, the Appellant provided arguments to overrule the decision in
the final report of the EO, followed by answers of the EO and the CPVO.
8. On 18 December 2014, the CPVO announced in its Decision R 1321 not to grant a CPVR to ‘Braeburn 78’
and provided arguments to underpin the decision.
9. The Appellant lodged an appeal against Decision R 1321 (Appeal A001/2015), dated 17 February 2015. The
Registry of the Board of Appeal (hereinafter also: the Registry and the BoA, respectively) confirmed this
appeal on 24 February 2015. The Grounds of Appeal were e-mailed to the CPVO on 30 April 2015. The
Registry acknowledged the receipt of the Grounds of Appeal on 6 May 2015. The Grounds of Appeal mainly
focused on the alleged distinctness of ‘Braeburn 78’ from ‘Royal Braeburn’, providing arguments re (i) the
colour, striation, size and shape of the fruits and (ii) harvest time and harvest date. The Appellant requested
3

that: (i) Decision R 1321 be set aside, (ii) the DUS test conducted by the EO be resumed for at least one
independent growth period or alternatively that (iii) the DUS test be taken over by a different testing
authority for at least two independent growth periods, (iv) an oral hearing be held before the testing
authority or the BoA and (v) witness, expert Prof. Bergamini be heard.
10. On 20 May 2015, the Rectification Committee of the CVPO reported to the Registry that it decided not to
rectify the contested decision. This decision was sent to the Appellant by the Registry on 27 May 2015
adding that the case was remitted to the BoA, taking up the case as Appeal Case A001/2015.
11. The CPVO refuted all arguments raised in the Grounds of Appeal of the Appellant in its submission provided
to the BoA on 15 September 2015 and requested the appeal be rejected as unfounded.
12. On 15 December 2015, on oral hearing was organized in Angers during which both the Appellant and the
CPVO presented their arguments. The focus of the debate was mainly (i) on the technical questions re the
fruit characteristics, the harvest time and the harvest date and (ii) on the suitability of the Angers region to
test ‘Braeburn’ apples.
13. By decision of 15 December 2015, the BoA dismissed the Appeal as not well-founded.
14. The Appellant started an action against the aforementioned decision of the BoA before the General Court
of the European Union. The Appellant put forward three pleas in law in support of its action, alleging,
respectively, in essence, (i) misuse of power and infringement of Article 57(3) of Regulation (EC) No 2100/94
(the Basic Regulation, BR), (ii) that the technical examination is vitiated by various errors and, finally, (iii)
infringement of the right to be heard and failure to state reasons.
15. By its judgement dated 5 February 2019 in Case T-177/16, the General Court annulled the decision of the
BoA.
In points 52-56, the General Court considered the following:
“52 The contested decision thus does not set out in a clear and unequivocal fashion the reasoning of the
Board of Appeal which led it to rely on the deficient findings of GEVES and to disregard the arguments put
forward by the applicant.
53 In that regard, it should also be noted that, as regards the reference variety, the table drawn up by
GEVES in support of its examination report (…) does not include, for 2012, any indication relating to the
starch regression index — even though the CPVO itself recognised that this was a key criterion —, to
firmness, acidity or the refractometric index respectively.
54 Moreover, the CPVO failed to provide any explanation making it possible to understand the absence of
those elements when questioned on the subject at the hearing.
55 This is also the case with regard to the precise date on which the CPVO carried out the examinations
relating to the characteristics of the ripe fruit for the Braeburn mutants, which does not appear in the GEVES
report and about which the CPVO was unable to provide clarification to the Court at the hearing.
56 It must therefore be considered that the reasoning which led the Board of Appeal to reject the arguments
raised by the applicant is so deficient that it must be held that the reasons on which the contested decision
is based have not been stated or are, at the very least, inadequate.”
16. On 15 April 2019, the Registry e-mailed the Appellant that the CPVO did not lodge an appeal against decision
T-177/16 of the General Court.
17. In its written observations dated 3 June 2019, the following forms of order were sought by the Appellant:
(i) It is requested that the proceedings to be continued and the application for a further technical
examination be referred back to the Office.
(ii) It is requested that this technical examination be carried out on both the new testing field 25 km north
of Angers and in Bellegarde/Provence.
(iii) It us further requested that the applicant be permitted to inspect these tests.
(iv) Finally, it is requested that the applicant be exempted from the examination fees for the further technical
examination. In the alternative, it is requested that the examination fees for 2010-2013 be refunded.
(v) For reasons of procedural economy, if the Board of Appeal wishes not to grant one or more of the above-
mentioned applications, a written notice is requested from the Board of Appeal with a time limit for
submissions for the parties involved.
(vi) Purely as a precautionary measure, oral proceedings pursuant to Article 77(1) of the Basic Regulation
are requested, and the hearing of Prof. Bergamini as technical expert (Article 78(1) of the Basis Regulation).
4

18. On 12 June 2019, the CPVO requested the chairman of the BoA to remit the case to the CPVO. The chairman
agreed with this request the same day. The Appellant was notified about this decision on 21 November
2019.
19. The Legal Unit of the CPVO asked additional information to the EO on 25 November 2019, more particularly
information re (i) the starch regression value for ‘Royal Braeburn’ in 2012 (indeed, there was no value in
the final report of the EO) and (ii) the exact dates on which the observations of the fruit at the moment of
eating maturity had been performed. The EO e-mailed its response to the CPVO on 11 December 2019. The
CPVO subsequently requested the EO to provide a copy of the excel file mentioned in the EO’s answers of
11 December 2019. This excel file (with fruit data of several varieties – including ‘Braeburn 78’ and ‘Royal
Braeburn’ - during a period from 2005 to 2013) was received by the CPVO on 28 January 2020. The data
and communications between the CPVO and the EO were provided to the Appellant on 6 July 2020, who
acknowledged the receipt thereof on 7 July 2020.
20. By letter of 4 September 2020 the Appellant submitted its comments to the aforementioned data and
communications and stated that the deficiencies in the technical examinations carried out in 2012 and 2013
could not be remedied with retroactive effect. Furthermore, the Appellant repeated its previous requests,
referred to in point 17 above.
21. By decision of 19 October 2020, the CPVO maintained its previous decision R 1321 to refuse granting plant
variety right protection for ‘Braeburn 78’, without the need to (i) perform a further technical examination;
and (ii) conduct an oral hearing.
22. On 17 December 2020, the Appellant lodged an appeal against the decision of the CPVO of 19 October 2020
(hereinafter referred to as ‘the Second Appeal’). On 19 February 2021 the Appellant submitted its grounds
of appeal in the Second Appeal proceedings to the CPVO, a document of 123 paragraphs grouped into six
major capita, which in essence contained the following:
- Caput I: Forms of order sought:
 (i) The Office’s decision referred to above (decision mentioned in point 21) be annulled for
infringement of Article 75 BR and the case be remitted to the department of first instance (competent
body of the Office) without further consideration of its merits (sentence 2 of Article 72 BR);
 (ii) The decision referred to above be annulled and the case be remitted for infringement of Article
75 and 76 BR to the department of first instance for the conduct of the technical examination at a
location other than Angers-Beaucouzé, in particular Bellegarde, France or Laimburg, Italy;
 (iii) The appeal fee be refunded (Article 83(4) BR);
 (iv) In the alternative, that oral proceedings be held in accordance with Article 77 BR.
- Caput II (paragraphs 1-23): Infringement of Article 75 BR - rejection of the request for oral proceedings;
- Caput III (paragraphs 24-36): Infringement of Article 75 BR – denial of the right to submit evidence;
- Caput IV (paragraphs 37-100): Various errors in the technical examination;
- Caput V (paragraphs 101-110): Misuse of powers and infringements of Article 57(3) BR and the principle
of equal treatment – re-examination on account of the unsuitable climate of the test location;
- Caput VI (paragraphs 111-123): Infringement of the principle of examination by the Office (Article 76 BR).
23. On 17 December 2020, the Rectification Committee of the CPVO performed a detailed interlocutory revision
of the appeal lodged by the Appellant. The decision of this revision (dated 12 March 2021) was not to rectify
the contested decision of 19 October 2020 and not to refund the appeal fee.
24. On 22 April 2021, the Second Appeal was remitted to the Board of Appeal.
25. On 19 May 2021, a new Board of Appeal was installed consisting of Mr. P. van der Kooij (as Chairman), Mr.
D. Reheul (as Rapporteur) and Mr. H. Koenraad (as Member).
26. In January 2022, the BoA requested its Registry to organize an oral hearing should the parties wish to be
heard. On 9 February 2022, the BoA confirmed that the oral hearing would take place on 17 May 2022.
27. On 15 April 2022, the BoA served on the parties a summons to attend the oral hearing to be held by video
conference (hereinafter “Oral Hearing”). The date and time of the Oral Hearing was set at 17 May 2022 at 9.30
a.m. The parties were informed, amongst other things, that a ‘dry run’ would be held on Monday 16 May 2022
at 9.00 a.m., to ensure the videoconference line and facilities functioned as required, before the Oral Hearing.
The parties were also invited, amongst other things, to provide pleading notes to the Board of Appeal before 13
May 2022.
5

28. On 29 April 2022, an internal e-mail was sent by the Technically Qualified Member of the BoA – in consultation
with the whole BoA - to the Registry, copying others, stating “The BOA in case A014/2020 (‘Braeburn 78’) wants
to submit a number of questions addressed to the CPVO Office and the Examination Office to be dealt with
during the hearing of 17 May 2022. You can find the questions in the attachment. Can you provide our questions
to these parties? Can you confirm the reception of these questions? Best regards [sender name and contact
details]”.
29. On 2 May 2022, the Registry sent the CPVO and the relevant EO an e-mail with an attachment containing a
number of technical questions (hereinafter “Board’s Technical Questions” or “BTQs”) and stating that the Board’s
Technical Questions were “to be dealt with during the oral hearing of 17 May 2022”.
30. On 13 May 2022, the Appellant sent the Registry an e-mail informing the Board of Appeal that the Appellant
was “considering” whether it attended the Oral Hearing or not. On the same date, the Registry sent an e-mail
to the Appellant requesting clarification on the Appellant’s communication. The Appellant subsequently sent the
Registry a second email at 16.10 p.m. with a letter attached. The e-mail stated, amongst other things, “Please
immediately pass on this letter to the Board, there is no need for a dry run [to ensure the videoconference line
and facilities functioned properly] on Monday morning”. The attached letter (hereinafter “Appellant Letter of 13
May 2022”) stated, amongst other things, that the Appellant “will not attend the oral proceedings and withdraws
the request for oral proceedings” and requested “a written decision on the basis of the previous factual
presentation and the following arguments” . The arguments related to (i) the infringement of the right to be
heard and (ii) the determination of the ripeness and maturity (in German: Pflückreife and Genussreife,
respectively).
31. On the same date, i.e. 13 May 2022, the Registry sent the CPVO an e-mail informing the CPVO that the Appellant
no longer wished to have an Oral Hearing, and providing the CPVO a copy of the Appellant’s Letter of 13 May
2022.
32. On the same date, i.e. 13 May 2022, at 17.59 p.m., the CPVO sent the Registry an email attaching 4 documents,
specifically: (1) 1 document containing written answers to the Board’s Technical Questions Document with 2
documents affixed as annexes (“CPVO’s Response to the BTQs”); and (2) a document entitled ‘CPVO Pleading
Notes’.
33. On 16 May 2022, the Registry sent the CPVO an e-mail setting the deadline for the CPVO to provide comments
on the Appellant’s Letter of 13 May 2022.
34. On the same date, i.e. 16 May 2022 at 12.31 p.m., the Registry sent the Appellant an e-mail stating “[f]or the
sake of completeness, the Board would like to share with you the CPVO answers to the Board’s questions prior
to the oral hearing”, and attaching the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs.
35. On the same date, i.e. 16 May 2022, the Appellant sent an e-mail to the Registry and to the CPVO, enclosing
one attachment. The attachment was a letter addressed to the Chairperson of the CPVO Board of Appeal and
raising an objection pursuant to Article 48 of the Basic Regulation (hereinafter “Objection Letter”).
36. On 17 May 2022, the Registry sent the Appellant an e-mail: (i) stating that “…[o]nly through your letter [i.e. the
Appellant’s Objection Letter], the Board became aware of the fact that this document [i.e. the Board’s Technical
Questions] was not sent to you before. This has clearly been caused by a human error for which the Board
apologises”; (ii) inviting the Appellant to provide observations on the Board’s Technical Questions and the CPVO’s
Response to the BTQs within 3 months; and (iii) stating that, by raising an objection pursuant to Article 48(3)
of the Basic Regulation there may be “a serious delay in the current proceedings” and requesting confirmation
from the Appellant that the Appellant wished to continue to raise the objection. On the same date, the Appellant
sent the Registry an e-mail stating that the objection it had raised pursuant to Article 48(3) of the Basic
Regulation remained “unchanged” and that the Appellant would “try to accommodate the 3-month time limit”.
37. On the same date, i.e. 17 May 2022, the Appellant submitted to the CPVO a request for public access to
documents concerning the case-file in Case A014/2020.
38. On 25 May 2022, the Registry sent the parties in the appeal proceedings an e-mail concerning the most recent
procedural developments and next steps in the main proceedings regarding Case A014/2020. The e-mail
informed the parties, amongst other things, that the Board of Appeal had: (i) provided the Appellant the Board’s
Technical Questions and the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs and invited the Appellant to provide observations on
those documents; and (ii) resolved not to include the document entitled ‘CPVO Pleading Notes’ and received by
6

the Registry from the CPVO on 16 May 2022, in the appeal case file, and that the document would not be
considered by the Board of Appeal, as no oral hearing was currently scheduled.
39. On 2 June 2022, the CPVO sent the Registry an e-mail requesting a time extension for the CPVO to provide
observations on the Appellant Letter of 13 May 2022.
40. On 7 June 2022, the Registry sent the CPVO an e-mail granting the CPVO a time extension until 18 July 2022
to provide observations on the Appellant Letter of 13 May 2022.
41. On 8 June 2022, the Registry sent the Appellant documents concerning the case-file in Case A014/2020.
42. On 18 July 2022, the CPVO sent the Registry an e-mail with a document attached. The attached document
contained the CPVO’s observations on the Appellant Letter of 13 May 2022. On the same day, the Registry
forwarded the observations to the Members of the BoA.
43. On 10 August 2022, the Appellant sent the Registry an e-mail with a letter attached, requesting a time extension
to the deadline for the Appellant to provide observations on the BTQs and the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs.
44. On 11 August 2022, the Registry sent the Appellant an e-mail informing the Appellant that the deadline for the
Appellant to provide observations on the Board’s Technical Questions and the CPVO’s Response to the BTQs,
had been extended.
45. On 31 August 2022, the Appellant sent the Registry an e-mail with a document attached. The document
contained the Appellant’s observations on the BTQs and on the replies of the CPVO and EO to the BTQs.
On the lodgement of the objection
46. As stated in point 35 above, on 16 May 2022 the Appellant lodged, with the Registry, an objection pursuant to
Article 48 of the Basic Regulation, by way of a letter. The objection was (solely) directed against the Chairperson
of the CPVO Board of Appeal.
47. On 20 May 2022, the Board of Appeal resolved to convene a panel of members to examine the objection
comprising Mr. M. Navin-Jones as Alternate Chairperson, Mr. H. Koenraad as the Legally Qualified Member and
Mr. D. Reheul as the Technically Qualified Member.
48. On 25 May 2022, the Registry sent the parties in the Second Appeal proceedings an e-mail concerning the
objection with an attachment. The e-mail and attachment: (i) informed the parties that an Article 48 BR objection
had been lodged; (ii) set out the action the Alternate Board intended to take following the lodgement of the
objection; and (iii) set out a number of questions addressed to each party concerning the objection (hereinafter
“Alternate Board’s Questions”).

53. On 26 August 2022, the Appellant provided the Registry its observations on the replies.

8

2. REASONS
2.1. On the admissibility of the appeal
59. The appeal complies with Articles 67, 68 and 69 BR. It is therefore admissible.
60. However, the appeal is not well-founded.
2.2. On the substance
2.2.1. Legal considerations
a. On the rejection of the request for oral proceedings (infringement of Article 75 BR; point
22, Caput I (iv) and II)
61. In Caput I (iv) and II of the Second Appeal, the Appellant claims the right to be heard in oral proceedings in
accordance with Article 77(1) BR, which was refused by the CPVO decision of 19 October 2020. In essence,
according to the Appellant it is irrelevant, from a legal point of view, whether the Office considers a requested
hearing to be necessary, thus, in the present case, a hearing had to take place because it was so requested.
62. According to the CPVO, confirming its Decision R 1321 to refuse the protection of the variety ‘Braeburn
78’, there was no need to perform a further technical examination as requested by the Applicant, nor an
oral hearing. Also according to the Rectification Committee, it was up to the Office to decide if it was
necessary to have a hearing or not.
63. The Board concurs with the CPVO on this point, on the basis of several provisions in the BR. Pursuant to
Article 75 BR decisions of the Office shall be accompanied by statements of the grounds on which they are
based. They shall be based only on grounds or evidence on which the parties to proceedings have had an
opportunity to present their comments orally or in writing. This implies that decisions of the Office can also
be taken solely on the basis of written documents and that, as a consequence, there is no obligation for
an oral hearing as requested by the Appellant. Moreover, there is a difference between the German text of
Article 77(1) BR on the one hand and the Dutch text on the other: the latter uses the word ‘kan’ (in
English: may) which seems to give more freedom to the Office with regard to this issue. Article 78(3) BR
further explains that if the Office considers it necessary that a party to proceedings, witness or expert give
evidence orally, it shall, among other things, issue a summons requiring the relevant person to appear
before it.
64. Anyway, by its e-mail of 15 April 2022, the Registry of the BoA made all necessary preparations and
arrangements for an oral hearing; in agreement with all parties, the date for an online meeting was fixed on 17
May at 9.30 a.m. The Registry of the BoA asked a list of participants to be provided before 5 May 2022.
Answering a reminder sent by the Registry on 6 May 2022, the Appellant sent a list of its participants, including
Prof. Bergamini as a technical expert.
65. Surprisingly, on 13 May 2022, the Appellant informed the Registry of the BoA not to attend the oral proceedings
and to withdraw the request for oral proceedings. Moreover, on 9 December 2022 the Appellant informed the
CPVO that it did not wish to apply unilaterally for oral proceedings.
66. Since the Appellant was given full possibility to defend its points of view in oral proceedings, but eventually
waived the opportunity to do so, its request for such oral proceedings lost its ground.
b. On the denial of the right to submit evidence/rejection of the request to hear Prof.
Bergamini (infringement of Article 75 BR; point 22, Caput I (i) and III)
67. Fundamentally, according to the Appellant, the right to submit evidence, including the request to hear Prof.
Bergamini, constituted a separate and distinct means of giving or obtaining evidence within the meaning of
Article 78(d) BR. It related to the new evidence presented by the Office (Excel spreadsheet and email
correspondence) and hence a new fact, on which Prof. Bergamini had not previously been able to submit
observations. It should therefore have been admitted as such and the evidence should have been subjected
to an assessment by the Office in oral proceedings, particularly as it was appropriate and relevant. The
9

Appellant further alleged that the Office incurred in a breach of the obligation to provide information, in
particular in the cover letter sent by the Office’s Legal Advisor by e-mail of 6 July 2020.
68. According to the CPVO, the previous “Parere pro veritate” of Prof. Bergamini enclosed with the observations
of 21 July 2014 made by the Appellant to the Office did not analyze the reference variety ‘Royal Braeburn’,
which clearly hindered its probative value. Moreover, Prof. Bergamini’s observations were not carried out at
the entrusted Examination Office, but in an unknown testing place, without providing information on the
test methods used by him. The Office based its decision R 1321 on the assessment made by the experts of
the entrusted EO. The Appellant failed to justify why such oral proceedings and the hearing of Prof.
Bergamini were relevant to the decision-taking of the Office. The Office had carried out an appraisal of the
evidence and the results of the technical examination and concluded not to further hear the parties to the
proceedings nor their own appointed experts. Regarding the letter of 6 July 2020, the Office recalled that
this informative letter merely served to accompany the substantive pieces of information being provided,
the latter being the core object under the obligation to provide information. The information provided
included the exchange of emails between the Office and the EO, and an Excel table, both evidences including
elements about which the General Court in case T-177/16 had requested the Office to delve into. According
to the Office the Appellant could exercise the right to be heard by submitting written comments in their
letter of 4 September 2020. All in all, the Office is of the opinion that it cannot be alleged that the Appellant
had his right to be heard breached pursuant to Article 75 BR. The Appellant submitted written observations
to the Office on 3 June 2019, and then on 6 July 2020 the Office forwarded the Appellant the required
documents and communications between the Office and the EO and invited the Appellant accordingly to
submit his comments thereon, where the Appellant availed himself of the opportunity by filing a letter on 4
September 2020.
69. The Board concurs with the reasoning of the CPVO on this point. Pursuant to Article 78(3) BR it is at the
discretion of the Office (‘if the Office considers it necessary’) to issue a summons requiring parties to
proceedings, witnesses or experts to appear before it. Also without having been summoned, the Appellant
(and with him Prof. Bergamini) had several opportunities over the years to submit their observations, as
referred to by the Office. Prof. Bergamini also attended the oral hearing of the Board in the First Appeal
case (case A001/2015) on 15 December 2015 (see points 9-13 of this decision); during that hearing an
expert report from his hand (analysing the climate in Angers and the suitability of the location of the EO)
was submitted by the Appellant (see points 8 and 8-1 of the decision of the Board in case A001/2015).
Finally, as mentioned in point 30 of this decision, the Appellant refused to participate in an already organized
oral hearing in Angers on 17 May 2022, for which meeting also Prof. Bergamini had been invited.
c. On the principle of examination by the Office (infringement of Article 76 BR; point 22,
Caput I (ii) and VI)
70. The Appellant holds that the Rectification Committee’s decision of 19 October 2020 essentially does not
satisfy the examination requirement laid down in Article 76 BR. According to the Appellant, in paragraphs
21-22 of Appendix B.10, the Committee failed to appreciate that the annulment of decision A001/2015 was
based precisely on the fact that the findings of the GEVES testing centre (which logically relate to facts)
were incomplete and hence cannot support the conclusion reached by the Committee. It should therefore
have been up to the Committee to fill the gaps (that are relevant to the conclusion) and make findings of
fact that go beyond the incomplete findings of the testing centre.
71. According to the CPVO, the Appellant should have accompanied the requests of an oral hearing and of the
taking of oral evidence by an appropriate set of allegations grounding such requests. In the absence of such,
the Office could not anticipate whether the oral hearing and taking of evidence could have an impact on its
decision-making process, what is being alleged by the Appellant only at a later stage, and what was not
alleged within the time limits provided by the Office for the Appellant to submit observations. The Office did
investigate the facts related to the conduct of the technical examination of its own motion and reached a
conclusion on the basis of the evidence provided by the Examination Office.
10

72. The Board is of the opinion that the CPVO examined the facts in accordance with Article 76 BR. As set out
in point 19 of this decision on instigation of the Office the EO has provided the Office with additional
information concerning the DUS tests performed over the years. The Board will further explain in points 73
et seq. of this decision why this information has been sufficient in order to come to the conclusion of the
Office that decision R 1321 must be upheld.
2.2.2. Technical considerations
a. New information
73. After the judgment of the General Court in case T-177/16 (5 February 2019), the CPVO requested and
eventually received additional information from the EO regarding (i) starch measurements around harvest
dates and (ii) regarding dates when the eating quality was examined. Furthermore, on 13 May 2022, the
Registry of the BoA received written answers to the BTQ’s.
b. On the testing location (point 22, Caput I (ii) and V)
74. In its Second Appeal, the Appellant in Caput I (ii) and V criticizes the Angers-Beaucouzé location as being
unsuitable for testing the candidate variety and subsequently requested the technical examination to be redone
at a location other than Angers-Beaucouzé, in particular in Bellegarde, France or in Laimburg, Italy. The
reason for this request is that, according to the Appellant, supported by information provided by apple expert
Prof. Bergamini, the weather and climatic conditions at the Angers-Beaucouzé testing site differ substantially
from the conditions in the area of origin of ‘Braeburn 78’; as a consequence “differences that are visible in
the area of origin are not visible in the DUS-testing site Angers-Beaucouzé”.
75. The CPVO rejected this request arguing that in line with opinions of fruit experts consulted by the CPVO,
the Administrative Council of the CPVO has decided in November 2003 to divide as from 2004, the DUS
testing of apples in the EU between the Bundessortenamt in Germany and GEVES in France. Varieties of the
‘Braeburn’ group have been assigned to GEVES. This was published as Announcement N°2/2004 of CPVO in
the Official Gazette of the CPVO N°1/2004 of 15 February 2004, with announcement N°2/2004 on p. 92 et
seq. of the said Gazette. Angers-Beaucouzé is the GEVES entrusted testing site for apple varieties.
76. It is common knowledge in plant breeding and variety testing that any phenotypical expression is the result
of an interaction of a genotype with its environment. Hence, phenotypic expressions of genotypes may differ
in different environments, e.g. because of different weather and climatic conditions. Therefore, the colour
expression of ‘Braeburn 78’ indeed may be different in its region of origin compared to Angers. As according
to the DUS examiners, the genotype/environment interaction in Angers is low, this testing site guarantees
a stable testing environment and repeatable results.
77. Moreover, given the necessity of the presence of a reference collection (which takes many years to establish
for fruit crops such as apples), it is technically infeasible to organize different ad hoc testing locations for
different (categories of) varieties. The Appellant was aware of this when filing its application and no
comments or requests for special conditions were included by the Appellant in the Technical Questionnaire
to its application.
78. The BoA notes that the General Court has in Case T-445/16 (‘Gala Schnico’, 2018) and Case T-182/20 (Gala
Perathoner, 2021) rejected similar claims to redo the DUS examination due to the alleged unsuitability of
the Angers-Beaucouzé testing site for apple varieties. In those cases the Court evoked the principles of equal
treatment, legal certainty, and transparency when testing varieties of the same species under the same
environmental conditions.
79. For these reasons, the BoA dismisses the claim of the Appellant to redo the DUS testing at another testing
location.
c. On the alleged various errors in the technical examination (point 22, Caput IV)
80. In Caput IV, the Appellant argues that various errors occurred during the technical examination. The Appellant
claims a breach of TP/14/2 and has remarks on the time of harvest and the time of eating maturity, the
duty to conduct the test for at least two growing cycles and on the substitution of missing data of the
reference variety ‘Royal Braeburn’ with data of other varieties.
11

On the time of harvest and time of eating maturity
81. The Appellant claimed that the candidate and reference variety can only be properly harvested in line with
TP/14/2 if both have reached a starch index that is optimum for ‘Braeburn’ and its mutants. In the final
DUS report of the EO, there was no starch regression for the reference variety in 2012.
82. The CPVO reminds that time of harvest (character N° 56 in TP/14/2) is defined as “the optimum time of
picking to achieve fruit in peak conditions for eating”. According to TP/14/2 it is visually assessed as a
quantitative characteristic with 9 states of expression ranging from very early (note 1) to very late (note 9).
The Appellant has described ‘Braeburn 78’ as “late” in the Technical Questionnaire. The CPVO refers to “the
fiche technique variétale of ‘Braeburn’ mutants” reporting that the time of harvest for ‘Braeburn’ mutants in
late zone, can be defined in a period ranging from 5 to 20 October. The CPVO further recalls that the
optimum peak of time for harvest can vary from one fruit to another in the same apple tree since all fruits
of an apple tree are not exposed in the same way to the sun (e.g. lower part of the tree versus higher part
or internal versus external part of the canopy). The General Court has confirmed in its judgment T-445/16
(re ‘Gala Schnico’, 2018) that assessing the best harvest time is not “exact science”. Hence its determination
is left to the experience of examiners at the entrusted EO. The CPVO argues that it is up to the expert indeed
to decide on a mean date and that all fruits may not reach exactly the same date of eating maturity in the
storage room, but it is clear that all fruits will have reached that date at the time of observations.
83. Given the assignment of apple varieties of the ‘Braeburn’ group to the testing station of Angers as from
2004 (see point 75) and given the testing of ‘Royal Braeburn’ as a candidate variety in 1998-2002, the
testing station of Angers-Beaucouzé had at least 10 years of experience with varieties of the ‘Braeburn’
group during the examination period of ‘Braeburn 78’ (establishment years 2010 and 2011 and observation
years with a required number of fruits, 2012 and 2013). This experience was further articulated in the excel
file sent by the EO to the CPVO on 28 January 2020: this excel file comprises data regarding observations
of varieties of the ‘Braeburn’ group (‘Braeburn’, ‘Royal Braeburn’, ‘Mariri Red’, ‘Ella’) during the period 2005-
2013 (see also points 19 and 87).
84. The CPVO noted that the starch regression method has been developed by the French Centre technique
interprofessionnel des fruits et légumes (Ctifl) in order to support (not to replace) the determination of the
optimum picking time. According to the Ctifl-publication, the first harvest of varieties of the ‘Braeburn’ group
in the Loire valley (where the Angers-Beaucouzé station is situated), falls in the second decade of October
when the starch regression shows values between 4 and 6 (in a scale of 1-10). Ctifl recommends that in
conditions of conservation under normal atmosphere, fruits are fit to be marketed from the end of the
autumn until the beginning of the winter. The “Fiche variétale” in the Ctifl manual “Pomme-Poire de la
récolte au conditionnement-Outils pratiques” (2004) recommends a first harvest of varieties of the ‘Braeburn’
group between 5-20 October at a starch regression value of (4)5-6(7) and a conservation under normal
atmosphere for about 150 days. Also, the Swiss profession commission for the examination of fruit varieties
in its “Fiche variétale ‘Braeburn’, 2007” recommends a harvest time when the starch regression falls within
values 4-6 (file:///C:/Users/dirkr/Downloads/535-29928-fr-pub.pdf, consulted 22 November 2022).
85. Both the Appellant, the BoA in its decision of 15 December 2015, and the General Court in its Judgment
dated 5 February 2019 have noticed that in the final report of the EO, values for the starch regression and
fruit characteristics were missing for the year 2012 for the reference variety ‘Royal Braeburn’. Apparently,
the oral hearing during the procedure of the First Appeal did not shed light on the reasons for this missing
value. Only from the answers, provided by the EO on 11 December 2019 upon a specific request of the
CPVO of 25 November 2019, did it become clear that values for starch regression on the reference variety
‘Royal Braeburn’ were not made since there were not enough fruits on the trees of ‘Royal Braeburn’ to make
a representative sample of 20 apples for destructive analyses. There were not enough fruits since there
were only 4 trees of ‘Royal Braeburn’ in the reference collection versus 10 trees for ‘Braeburn 78’. According
to the CPVO, the presence of 4 trees of the reference variety ‘Royal Braeburn’ versus 10 trees of ‘Braeburn
78’ is not an issue since only for varieties under test, 10 trees are requested (TP/14/2), not for reference
varieties.
86. In its e-mail of 25 November 2019 to the EO, the CPVO further asked, in case there were no values for
starch regression of ‘Royal Braeburn’ in 2012, to indicate in which years ‘Royal Braeburn’ was used for
reference purposes and to give some examples of starch regression in those years with state of expression;
this may help to confirm – in addition to the official description of 2002 for ‘Royal Braeburn’ – that the
examiner had sufficient experience to acquire data for 2012.
87. On 28 January 2020, the EO provided the CPVO an excel file with values for starch regression and
measurements of fruit characteristics in a time window 2005-2013 of 40 observations on varieties of the
12

‘Braeburn’ group, namely ‘Ella’, ‘Mariri Red’, ‘Braeburn’, ‘Royal Braeburn’ and ‘Braeburn 78’. From 2010 on,
starch analysis was always conducted the day prior to the harvest date or on the harvest day; harvest days
ranged from 30 September to 16 October (a window of about 2 weeks); the mean value for 17 starch
regressions was 6,1 with single outliers 5.3 and 8.1; dates for measurements for colour fell in a time window
from 14 to 22 November.
88. In 2012, the varieties ‘Braeburn’, ‘Ella’ and ‘Mariri Red’ produced enough fruits to conduct a starch analysis.
According to the CPVO, the GEVES examiners know that ‘Braeburn’, ‘Ella’ and ‘Mariri Red’ have the same
period of harvest maturity as ‘Royal Braeburn’. The experts of the EO confirm in their email of 11 December
2019 that ‘Ella’ and ‘Mariri Red’ are known to have the same period of harvest maturity as ‘Royal Braeburn’.
Since the trees of ‘Ella’ were planted in the same year as the trees of ’Braeburn 78’ it is acceptable that
data from ‘Ella’ were used as a proxy for ‘Royal Braeburn’ to deliver supportive information regarding the
harvest date. Values for starch regression for ‘Ella’ and ‘Braeburn 78’ were 6.6 and 5.5 respectively on 10
October 2012, 1 day prior to the harvest. These values are close to the optimal values.
89. In conclusion, the starch regression gives supplementary, supportive information for determining the optimal
harvest time, as harvest time is determined visually as laid down in TP/14/2, leaning on the experience of
the examiners. As already noted, it is to be assumed that the examiners have extensive experience with
testing varieties of the ‘Braeburn’ group. Taking this into account and given the extra information provided
in the EO’s email of 11 December 2019 and the accompanying excel table provided on 28 January 2020, the
BoA finds it acceptable that data from ‘Ella’ were used as a proxy for ‘Royal Braeburn’. As these data were
available during two growing seasons with enough fruits of ‘Braeburn 78’ to conduct all analyses in line with
the Technical Protocol, the CPVO argues that there was no need to study an extra growing season.
90. Putting everything together, the BoA is of the opinion that missing values for starch regression for the
reference variety ‘Royal Braeburn’ in 2012 and using instead values of other ‘Braeburn’ mutants as proxy
values for ‘Royal Braeburn’ cannot be a point of discussion since the values for starch regression are not
necessary to decide on the harvest date because starch regression is not included in TP/14/2 and since they
provide only supporting information. Therefore, there was no need to conduct the DUS test during a further
examination year.
91. The BoA therefore dismisses the Appellant’s claim that “the candidate and reference variety can only be
properly harvested in line with TP/14/2 if both have reached a starch index that is optimum for ‘Braeburn’
and its mutants”.
On the coincidence of an equal harvest date (11 October) both in 2012 and 2013 and the allegation
that the growing seasons of 2012 and 2013 were very different
92. The Appellant is surprised that the harvest date coincided both in 2012 and 2013 although according to his
opinion the climate conditions in both growing periods were very different. The CPVO denies the claim that
the growing conditions were completely different between 2012 and 2013. Both parties used weather data,
retrieved from
https://www.meteociel.com/climatologie/obs_villes.php?code2=7230&mois=10&annee=2012
to defend their position. They used data from the whole year or data from specific months. However, neither
party explained why and how weather data might influence the harvest date.
93. Analyzing weather data for Angers-Beaucouzé
(https://www.meteociel.com/climatologie/obs_villes.php?code2=7230&mois=10&annee=2012; consulted
10-14 December 2022) with special emphasis on the period 11 September up to the harvest date of 11
October, learns the following:
Total annual precipitation was 750 mm in 2012 and 722 mm in 2013. The total sunshine for 2012 was 1147
hours versus 1164 hours for 2013. On an annual basis 2012 and 2013 were very similar for sunshine and
rainfall, but the Appellant is right to claim that this is quite irrelevant: what counts are the data during the
growing period and particularly during the month(s) immediately after the end of flowering and the summer
and autumn period up to the harvest date.
94. Several authors indicate the effect of temperature (e.g. expressed as heat units (see below)) in a period of
30-40 days after full bloom on harvest maturity: indices for starch and flesh firmness were significantly
higher when maximum and minimum temperatures in early development were higher, while this was not
the case for the background colour index (Warrington et al. 1999, studying among others ‘Braeburn’).
Warrington et al. (1999) further write: “it is likely that maturity and its associated biochemical processes are
13

influenced more directly by conditions near harvest” in line with the knowledge that the weather in the
month prior to the harvest is very important.
95. Heat units or Growing Degree Days (GDD) are a plant physiological meaningful parameter to assess plant
growth and development. For each day, the value of GDD was calculated as the difference between the
daily mean air temperature (measured in a weather station) and a base temperature which is according to
Lahaye et al. (2001) 7°C for apples. If the minimum temperature drops below the base temperature is it
replaced by the base temperature. The cumulative GDD’s obtained during a certain growing period are
calculated as the sum of GDD’s during that period and are called GDD. Calculating GDD is a standardized
method in agronomy to express differences in growth and development between years. Plant species need
a minimum quantity of GDD to reach the optimum harvest date; this quantity may differ among varieties.
96. According to Legave et al. (2009) apple flowering in Angers starts around 1 April: so the period 30-40 days
after full bloom spans April and the first half of May.
97. The BoA calculated a number of data as included below in Table 1.
Table 1: Analysis of weather data in Angers-Beaucouzé in 2012 and 2013
GDD Sunshine (h) Precipitation (mm)
°C days
1 April-15 May 2012 222.7 (100)* 260.8 (100) 138.3 (100)
2013 220.9 (99) 251.3 (96) 56 (40)
11 September-11 October 2012 272,3 (100) 141.3 (100) 97 (100)
2013 300.3 (110) 128.6 (91) 55.3 (57)
*relative values between brackets
The values for GDD and sunshine2 in the period 1 April-15 May were very similar in 2012 and 2013. It rained
far more in 2012 than in 2013 in that period, but precipitation was not growth-limiting.
In a period of 30 days prior to the harvest date, there was about 10% difference both in GDD and sunshine
but in opposite direction. It rained far more in 2012 than in 2013 but precipitation was not growth-limiting.
98. In conclusion, weather conditions always differ between years and these differences may influence growth
and development. But both GDD and sunshine in the crucial periods early after flowering and close to the
harvest date were not very different between 2012 and 2013.
Knowing that the harvest dates of varieties of the ‘Braeburn’ group in the years 2005 up to 2013 (with
certainly very different weather conditions) varied between 30 September and 16 October (see point 93),
given the recommendation in the “fiche technique variétale of ‘Braeburn’ mutants” to harvest ‘Braeburn’
mutants grown in the “zone tardive” (late region) between 5 and 20 October (see point 82) and given the
similar weather conditions in the crucial periods of the year in 2012 and 2013, it is not beyond expectations
that harvest dates coincided in both years.
99. Therefore, the BoA dismisses the claim of the Appellant that it was not correct and not credible to harvest
the fruit of ‘Braeburn 78’ on the same date in 2012 and 2013.
On the analysis of eating maturity (colour analysis)
100. The Appellant claims that the apples of ‘Braeburn 78’ were not picked at the optimal dates; he further
disputes that the results obtained at eating maturity are robust, since the optimal harvest ripeness is an
essential prerequisite for the correct determination of eating maturity. The CPVO is of the view “that the
two notions should be interpreted separately and on its own respective merits”.
101. Time of eating maturity is defined in the CPVO technical protocol as “the period when a fruit has reached
optimum colour, firmness, texture, aroma and flavour for consumption. Depending on the type of fruit,
this period can occur directly after removal from the tree (e.g. early varieties) or after a period of storage
or conditioning (e.g. later varieties)”. Time of eating maturity may differ according to the atmospheric
circumstances during the conservation. The CPVO mentions that the EO conserves its apples under
conditions of a normal atmosphere (referring to ambient concentrations of O2 and CO2).
2 Light (with sunshine as a proxy) is partly responsible for the colouring of apples.
14

102. According to the Ctifl information fruits of ‘Braeburn’ are fit to be marketed from the end of the autumn
until the beginning of the winter. Ctifl recommends a conservation period under normal atmosphere for
about 150 days (see point 84). This information is in line with what the examiners write in their e-mail of
11 December 2019, namely that commercialization of fruits from ‘Braeburn’ in the Loire Valley starts at
the beginning of November and that any date from November to March (i.e. a time span of 150 days) is
appropriate for the assessment of harvest maturity (they probably mean eating maturity).
103. The information sent by the EO to the CPVO on 11 December 2019 regarding observations in 2012 and
2013 further mentions that colour analysis was conducted on 16 November 2012 and colour description
between 16 and 27 November 2012 (the exact date was not recorded). On 28 November 2012 observations
were made with the experts during the DUS commission. In 2013 colour analysis was conducted on 13
November and colour description between 13 November and 3 December (exact date not recorded).
Observations with the experts during the DUS commission were made on 3 December.
104. This means that these analyses were conducted at least one month after the harvest date which was 11
October both in 2012 and 2013 and thus within the recommended time period to conduct them.
105. The Appellant further argues that the final DUS report mentions single colorimetric values and that it is
unclear how these values correspond to the scores 1-7 given in the Technical Protocol to assess pattern
of over colour. He further argues that the report does not mention what device was used (to conduct
colorimetric analyses) and that no standard errors on the data were provided. The CPVO again mentions
that, according to the Technical Protocol, colour is a visually observed characteristic and that
measurements with an apparatus provide only supportive data. A difference of 2 degrees, obtained with
the Minolta apparatus, has been defined as perceived by the human eye. As values between ‘Braeburn 78’
and its closest resembling varieties in the table, presented in the final DUS report (see point 6) differ less
than 2 units, the supportive data underpin the visually obtained conclusion that regarding colour ‘Braeburn
78’ was not distinct from its resembling varieties.
106. The claims of the Appellant that the dates to assess the time of eating maturity were wrong and that the
provided data were unclear, are therefore unfounded and dismissed.
107. It follows from all the above considerations that the claim of the Appellant with regard to the refunding
of the appeal fee (point 22, Caput I (iii)) also has to be dismissed.
3. ON THE COSTS
108. For the reasons set out above, the Board finds that the appeal herein is not well-founded and decision
R1321 is upheld.
109. As the losing party to an appeal bears the costs thereof, pursuant to Article 85(1) of Council Regulation
2100/94, the costs shall be borne by the Appellant.
4. ON LEGAL REMEDIES
110. Pursuant to Article 73 BR an appeal may be brought before the Court of Justice against this decision
within two months of its service.
P. VAN DER KOOIJ D. REHEUL
Chairman of the Board of Appeal Rapporteur
15
