parties: Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. v. CPVO
title: CPVO BoA A001/2022 - Queen
text:
CPVO BOARD OF APPEAL DECISION DATED 13 DECEMBER 2023
in Case No. A001/2022 regarding
Community Plant Variety Right Title No. 33910 (Variety
denomination ‘Queen’, Species ‘Citrus reticulata Blanco‘)
Concerning the appeal lodged by:
Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. Appellant
Camino del Iryda s/n
03190 Pilar de la Horadada (Alicante)
Spain
Represented by:
Mr Vicente Molés Vilar
C/ Pascual y Genis num 19 Piso 3 ̊
46002 Valencia
Spain
Against a decision of:
Community Plant Variety Office CPVO
3 Boulevard Maréchal Foch,
49000 Angers
France
Represented by:
Ms Angela Martínez Lopez, Ms Montserrat García-Moncó Fuente and others
With the Intervention of:
Seedless Gold International S.L. Intervener
C/Cabo Salou 19
30720 San Javier (Murcia)
Spain
Represented by:
Mr Pedro Bermúdez Belmar
Plaza Alfonso el Magnánimo 12-Bajo
46003 Valencia
Spain

On 11 February 2022, Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. (hereinafter: the “Appellant”) lodged with the Registry of the
CPVO Board of Appeal (hereinafter: the “Registry”) a Notice of Appeal in relation to the registered transfer of the
Community plant variety right (“PVR”) Title No. 33910, variety denomination ‘Queen’, species Citrus reticulata
Blanco (hereinafter: “Queen”) to Seedless Gold International S.L. (hereinafter: “SGI”) and related action.
The CPVO Board of Appeal, composed of Mr Marcus Navin-Jones as Alternate Chairperson, Mr Hidde Koenraad as
the Legally Qualified Member, and Mr Willy Dillen as the Technically Qualified Member, gives the following Decision:
I. FORM OF ACTION SOUGHT
The Appellant requests the Board of Appeal to, amongst other things:
(i) annul the decision to register the transfer of the PVR for Title No. 33910 concerning
variety QUEEN (“Queen PVR”) from the Appellant to SGI;
(ii) declare the transfer of the Queen PVR, including the deletion of the Appellant’s details
in the register of Community Plant Variety Rights (the Register) regarding the Queen
PVR, as null and void; and
(iii) order that the Register is rectified and amended so as to ensure that the Appellant, not
SGI, is listed as the titleholder and owner of the Queen PVR, and the Appellant’s details
are entered into the Register as the titleholder for the Queen PVR.
The CPVO contends, together with the Intervener, that the appeal is inadmissible. In the event the appeal
is regarded as admissible, the CPVO, together with the Intervener, contend that the appeal is not well
founded and should be dismissed.
II. SUMMARY OF THE FACTS
1. On 21 April 2006, the Appellant submitted an application to the CPVO for a PVR concerning the species
Citrus reticulata Blanco with the proposed variety denomination ‘Queen’. The application was submitted
by Mr Renán de Teresa Almenara (“Mr Renán”) acting on behalf of the Appellant. Mr Renán was appointed
to represent the Appellant before the CPVO as regards certain issues pursuant to a form dated 12 April
2006.
2. On 17 December 2012, the CPVO granted the Appellant a PVR for the variety ‘Queen’.
3. On 15 February 2013, the relevant details of the Queen PVR were published in Gazette No. 1/2013.
4. On 23 September 2021, Mr Renán sent an email to the CPVO informing the CPVO that there was an
intention to request the CPVO to register the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant company to
a third party entity. The email contained a query as to what documentation should be provided to
effectuate the registration of the transfer and asking: “As a registered variety, which model should I send?
A or B?”. On the same date, i.e. 23 September 2021, the CPVO sent an email to Mr Renán stating ‘Please
use option B’.
5. On 24 September 2021, Mr Renán sent an email with an attachment to the CPVO requesting that the
CPVO register the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI. The email stated that the
attachment was “the contract between the companies”. The attachment was a 3 paged document dated
‘03/2021’.
6. On 25 September 2021, the CPVO sent an email to Mr Renán stating amongst other things: ‘Further to
your telephone conversation with my colleague, […], please find attached a template and a note on
assignments. To register a new applicant, the Office would need to receive a deed of assignment whereby
the present applicant transfers the CPVRs in question to the new applicant. Such deed should duly be
dated and signed by both persons. You may want to use our enclosed template and return it completed
via MyPVR.’
7. On 29 September 2021 at 11.08 am, Mr Renán sent an email to the CPVO with a document attached. The
document attached was a copy of the template document concerning the registration of transfers, initially
provided by the CPVO on 25 September 2021, containing certain information. The document indicated
that the request to register the transfer of the Queen PVR was being made by Mr Renán acting as the
2

representative of the transferor entity (namely the Appellant company) and by Mr Renán acting as the
representative of the transferee entity (namely SGI).
8. On the same date, i.e. 29 September 2021, the CPVO updated the CPVO Register. More specifically, the
CPVO updated some of the particulars relating to the Queen PVR in the Register so as to indicate that
SGI was the rightsholder for the Queen PVR, not the Appellant.
9. On the same date, i.e. 29 September 2021, the CPVO sent an invoice to SGI for payment of the official
fees (€100) for the registered transfer of the Queen PVR.
10. On 5 October 2021, the CPVO sent an email and letter to SGI which, amongst other things: (1) confirmed
receipt of the email from Mr Renán to the CPVO of 29 September 2021; (2) stated that the CPVO had
registered the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI; and (3) stated that the registered
transfer would be published in the Official Gazette No. 202106 of 15 December 2021.
11. On 6 October 2021, the CPVO accepted receipt of payment from SGI in respect of the registration of the
transfer.
12. On 7 October 2021, the CPVO’s deputy accountant informed Mr Emilio Teresa Almenara (“Mr Teresa”) of
the Appellant company, that the CPVO had accepted receipt of payment from SGI in respect of the
registration of the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI.
13. On 8 October 2021, the Appellant emailed the CPVO’s accounting department informing the CPVO,
amongst other things, that “…the instructions that [the CPVO] had received to make this transfer, were
not in any way communicated by [the Appellant company]”. Several documents were attached to that
communication including an “escritura” [deed] stating that Mr Teresa had been appointed CEO of the
Appellant company as of 24 August 2021. On the same date, i.e. 8 October 2021, the Appellant sent the
CPVO a further email with attachments. The email stated that the Appellant had been the registered
rightsholder of the Queen PVR as of December 2012, and had been paying the annual fee to the CPVO
as of February 2013. It also stated SGI had appeared to have paid for the annual fee for the Queen PVR
for the term 2021-2022, but that the annual period for the term 2020-2021, which had been paid for by
the Appellant, expired on 16 December 2021.
14. On 13 October 2021, the CPVO sent the Appellant an email informing the Appellant that the CPVO had
experienced technical difficulties in opening the attachments to the Appellant’s email of 8 October 2021,
and requesting the Appellant to re-send the attachments if necessary. On the same date, i.e. 13 October
2021, the Appellant re-sent the relevant attachments to the CPVO.
15. On 15 October 2021, the CPVO sent the Appellant an email informing the Appellant that, at the time the
CPVO registered the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI the CPVO “…had not been
informed of the changes in the board of directors of the [Appellant company]…” and that, at that time,
the CPVO “had received documents that, according to the data in its registry, facilitated the change of
ownership over the variety in question [from the Appellant to SGI]”. The email also informed the Appellant
that, in order to “re-assign” the ownership of the variety as recorded in the Register from SGI to the
Appellant, the Appellant should provide a number of documents including:
“- The attached form through which Seedless Gold International S.L. (as owner and assignor) assigns to Angel Teresa
Hermanos S.A. (as assignee) the ownership of the Community plant variety right on the Queen variety (file no.
2006/0947). The document must be duly completed and signed by both parties, Mr. Renan de Teresa Almenara in the
name and on behalf of Seedless Gold International S.L. and Mr. Emilio Teresa Almenara in name and by account of
Angel Teresa Hermanos S.A.
- Copy of the deed of termination and appointment of members of the board of directors of the company Angel Teresa
Hermanos S.A., which you have provided us with the signatures of the interested parties.
- An extract from the Mercantile Registry stating the change in the company's administrative body.”
16. On the same date, i.e. 15 October 2021, the Appellant sent an email to the CPVO stating, amongst other
things, that the Appellant had never transferred the ownership of the Queen PVR to any third party, and
that any document which had been provided to the CPVO purporting to agree to the registered transfer
was “devoid of total validity” since it had been “conceived with a clear criminal intent” which was to
misappropriate the Queen PVR illegally and to “steal it from its legitimate owners”. The Appellant
requested the CPVO to provide the Appellant with all the “forms and contracts” that the CPVO had been
3

provided to effectuate the registration of the transfer. The Appellant also requested the CPVO to
“immediately suspend the ownership of the QUEEN variety in favour of the company [SGI]” including any
action to give the impression that the owner of the Queen PVR was SGI.
17. On 18 October 2021, the CPVO sent the Appellant an email informing the Appellant that the matter was
“being evaluated by [the CPVO] legal department”. On the same date, i.e. 18 October 2021, the Appellant
sent an email to the CPVO repeating its request for the forms and contracts which the CPVO relied on to
effectuate the registration of the transfer.
18. On 19 September 2021, the CPVO sent the Appellant an email stating that “as we have indicated in a
previous email, the Office has received documents that, according to the data in its records on 29
September 2021, met the requirements established to register the assignment of a community plant
variety right for the variety in question. In particular, the documents have been provided by [Mr Renán],
who is registered as CEO and representative of Angel Teresa Hermanos S.A. according to the information
provided to the office”. At the same time, the CPVO also invited the Appellant to “take account of Article
80 of Regulation (EC) No 874/2009” and informed the Appellant that the Appellant could “…oppose the
change of ownership…” and “…submit a request for the deletion of an entry in the registration…” by
submitting an application, including relevant documentation supporting the application. The CPVO noted
in this regard that the Appellant could submit to the CPVO, pursuant to Article 80 of Regulation (EC) No
874/2009, documentation such as the documentation Mr Teresa had already provided to the CPVO “…that
certifies that on 29 September 2021…[Mr Renán]…was not authorised to act in the name and on behalf
of the [Appellant company]”. The CPVO also informed the Appellant that the Appellant’s access to
document request would be processed in accordance with Regulation 1049/2001.
19. On 19 October 2021, the Appellant sent the CPVO an email raising certain issues concerning the
Appellant’s request for access to documents.
20. On 21 October 2021, the CPVO sent an email to the Appellant informing the Appellant, amongst other
things, that the CPVO had responded to the Appellant’s request for access to documents.
21. On 27 October 2021, the Appellant submitted a request to the CPVO pursuant to Article 80 of Commission
Regulation (EC) No 874/2009 of 17 September 2009 establishing implementing rules for the application
of Council Regulation (EC) No 2100/94 as regards proceedings before the Community Plant Variety Office
(hereinafter “Implementing Rules Regulation” or “IRR”). That request was for the CPVO to, amongst other
things, re-register the transfer of the Queen PVR back to the Appellant. The request stated, amongst
other things, that: the original registration of the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI
was ‘null and void’ and that Mr Renán had no authorisation to request or effectuate the registration of
the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI, as Mr Renán did not have legal authority or
legal capacity to represent or act on behalf of the Appellant.
22. On 25 November 2021, the Appellant sent a communication to the CPVO informing the CPVO that: (1) on
3 November 2021, the Appellant had initiated criminal legal proceedings in Spain against both Mr Renán
and SGI for falsehood in commercial documents, forgery in a private document, unfair administration and
misappropriation; and that (2) in accordance with a Spanish court order of 16 November 2021 these
criminal proceedings were found to be admissible.
23. On 14 December 2021, the CPVO sent an email and letter to SGI and the Appellant. The letter was entitled
‘Request for deletion of an entry in the register – suspension of the case’, and stated, amongst other
things:
‘On 29 September 2021, the Office registered the transfer of the Community plant variety right for the variety
‘Queen’ of the species Citrus reticulata Blanco, registered under file No 2006/0947, from Ángel Teresa
Hermanos S.A. to Seedless Gold International S.L.
The transfer of ownership was registered on the basis of the document provided on 29 September 2021 (model
ES — option B), signed by Mr Renan de Teresa Almenara, as a person authorised to act in the name and on
behalf of both parties, namely the transferor and the transferee, in accordance with the data in the Office’s
register.
4

On 27 October 2021, the Office received a request for removal from the register (hereinafter ‘the application’)
submitted by Mr Moles Vilar, representing the company Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. (‘the applicant’), on the
basis of Article 80 of Commission Regulation (EC) No 874/2009 (‘the Rules of Procedure’ or ‘RP’).
In his application, the applicant states that the transfer of Community plant variety rights for the variety in
question had not been authorised by the transferor, Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A., on the ground that, contrary
to the information communicated and entered in the register of the Office, Mr Renan de Teresa Almenara
would not have the legal capacity to represent that transferor.
On this basis, the applicant requires the deletion of the registration of the change of ownership, so that it is
Angel Teresa Hermanos S.A. who is the holder of the Community plant variety right for the variety in question.
The applicant also provides documentation in support of his request for removal from the register.
On 25 November 2021, Mr Moles Vilar notified the Office of the lodging of a criminal complaint before the
Juzgado de Instrucción de Orihuela against Mr Renan de Teresa Almenara and the company Seedless Gold
International S.L. The aim of the criminal complaint coincided substantially with the present proceedings for
the removal of an entry from the register, in so far as it was intended to verify, inter alia, that Mr Renan de
Teresa Almenara lacked legal capacity to represent the company Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. at the time of
submitting the application for a change of ownership for the ‘Queen’ variety.
Since the conclusions of the criminal proceedings before the national courts are strong for the Office to be able
to take a decision in accordance with the procedure under Article 80 RP, and considering that the national
judicial authorities are in a better position to conduct a thorough investigation of the facts, the Office considers
it necessary and hereby orders that those proceedings be stayed on the basis of the principles of economy,
efficiency and effectiveness of administrative action.
The transfer of the Community plant variety right from Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. to Seedless Gold
International S.L. is prima facie justified on the basis of the documentation provided to the Office and is
therefore governed by a presumption of validity. Consequently, the Office will maintain the register of Seedless
Gold International S.L. as owner and the change of ownership will follow the publication course in the Official
Gazette.
As soon as the procedures before the national authorities have been completed without delay, the Office will
be in a position to take a decision with regard to the request for deletion of an entry in the Register and, in
the event that the decision would result in the subsequent change in ownership of the variety, such a change
would be duly reflected in the Register and published in the Official Gazette. Finally, the Office ensures its
compliance with the principles of administrative and judicial cooperation enshrined in Article 91 of Council
Regulation No 2100/94, and remains at the disposal of the Spanish judicial authorities for any request for
information or documentation they deem necessary.’
24. On 15 December 2021, there was a publication in the Gazette concerning the Queen PVR, informing of
the change in the name of the rightsholder and other related details from the Appellant to SGI.
25. On 24 March 2022, the Juzgado de Instrucción No. 2 de Orihuela, Spain sent the CPVO an order,
instructing ‘the suspension of the proceedings for the transfer or transfer of rights of the Queen variety
which is being carried out by Seedless Gold International S.L’ in the context of the pending criminal
complaint proceedings against SGI and Mr Renán.
26. On 20 May 2022, the CPVO sent a response letter to the Juzgado de Instrucción No. 2 de Orihuela in
Spain, replying to the court’s order received on 24 March 2022. In its letter, the CPVO stated, amongst
other things, that: (1) the CPVO had included, in the Register, information on the existence of the pending
criminal proceedings in Spain; and that (2) the CPVO was not in a position to carry out the interim
suspension as requested by the Spanish court, since, according to the CPVO, the procedure of registering
the transfer of the Queen PVR was not a pending issue, as was implied in the order, but an issue which
had already been effectuated and concluded.
27. On 15 June 2022, details of the Spanish criminal proceedings were published in the Gazette 3/2022,
stating ‘Court of Instruction n◦2 - Orihuela – ES Preliminary proceedings - N◦002286/2021’ and ‘Offence
of forgery of a commercial and private document, unfair administration and misappropriation’.
III. PROCEDURE BEFORE THE BOARD OF APPEAL
28. On 11 February 2022, the Appellant lodged with the Registry, a Notice of Appeal concerning the registered
transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI.
5

29. On 2 March 2022, SGI informed the Registry that it wished to participate in the appeal proceedings
concerning Case A001/2022 (Queen).
30. On 31 March 2022, the appeal was remitted to the Board of Appeal.
31. On 13 April 2022, the Appellant submitted to the Registry its Grounds of Appeal.
32. On 15 April 2022, details of Board of Appeal Case A001/2022 (Queen) were published in the Gazette
2/2022, stating ‘Request to delete information in the Register (change of holder)’.
33. On 10 June 2022, SGI submitted to the Registry its first observations on the Appellant’s Notice of Appeal
and Grounds of Appeal.
34. On 15 June 2022, the CPVO submitted to the Registry its first observations on the Appellant’s Notice of
Appeal and Grounds of Appeal.
35. On 3 August 2022, the Appellant submitted its reply observations to the Registry responding to the first
observations of both the CPVO and SGI.
36. On 11 October 2022, the Board of Appeal invited the CPVO and SGI to: (1) provide rejoinder observations
in response to the Appellant’s reply observations dated 3 August 2022; and (2) to respond to the questions
from the CPVO Board of Appeal addressed to them respectively, and provide any other relevant and
related observations concerning these questions.
37. On 14 October 2022, the CPVO requested a time extension of 4 months to submit its rejoinder
observations, and answers to the questions of the Board of Appeal.
38. On 20 October 2022, the Board of Appeal granted the requested time extension and informed the parties.
39. On 13 March 2023, SGI submitted its rejoinder observations and written answers to the Registry of the
CPVO Board of Appeal.
40. On 14 March 2023, CPVO submitted its rejoinder observations and written answers to the Registry of the
CPVO Board of Appeal.
41. On 13 June 2023, the Appellant provided written observations on the CPVO and SGI responses to the
Board of Appeal questions. The Appellant also informed the Board of Appeal that it did not request an
oral hearing.
42. On 15 June 2023, the CPVO requested an oral hearing.
43. On 8 August 2023, the CPVO Board of Appeal served on the parties a summons to attend an oral hearing.
44. On 20 September 2023, the oral hearing was held, following which the oral proceedings were closed.
IV. ADMISSIBILITY
45. The CPVO asserts, together with the Intervener, that the appeal is inadmissible for, amongst others, the
following reasons.
46. First, according to the CPVO, the Appellant has limited its appeal to the ‘decision dated 14 December
2021’ (“14 December 2021 Act”). According to the CPVO, in doing so, the Appellant has thereby limited
and restricted the subject matter of this appeal to the 14 December 2021 Act. At the same time, and
according to the CPVO, the 14 December 2021 Act is not a measure against which an appeal may be
brought before the CPVO Board of Appeal. The CPVO argues that the 14 December 2021 Act does not
constitute a final decision of the Office, but a non-appealable administrative act of procedure. More
specifically, the CPVO states that the purpose of the 14 December 2021 Act was to suspend and stay the
proceedings initiated pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation. The CPVO takes the
view that, as the 14 December 2021 Act does not bring an end to the procedure for removing the
6

registration of the transfer of the Queen PVR, the 14 December 2021 Act is not a measure against which
an appeal may be brought before the CPVO Board of Appeal and therefore must be regarded as
inadmissible. The CPVO further argues that, should the appeal be considered admissible, this would imply
that the Board of Appeal would have the task of carrying out an assessment of facts and of law which the
CPVO has not yet been able to carry out in the first instance. This, according to the CPVO, would be
contrary to the principles of procedural law as laid down in European case law.
47. Second, according to the CPVO, the Appellant has not appealed, directly or indirectly, the “Office’s
communication of 5 October 2021”. That is because, according to the CPVO, it may be inferred that the
Appellant cites the ‘change of ownership agreement’ as a substantive element addressed in that decision,
and the ‘publishing’ of the agreement as a consequence of the effects of that ‘decision’. This means,
according to the CPVO, that the Appellant does not directly or indirectly appeal the Office’s communication
of 5 October 2021.
48. Third, according to the CPVO, the Appellant cannot appeal against the publication in the Official Gazette
of 15 December 2021 as this does not constitute an appealable decision under Article 67 of Council
Regulation (EC) No 2100/94 (“Basic Regulation”).
49. The Appellant contests the arguments advanced by the CPVO and asserts the appeal is admissible.
Findings of the Board of Appeal
50. Before assessing whether the conditions of admissibility are met in this appeal case, the Board of Appeal
finds it necessary, as a preliminary point, to address the issue of proceedings initiated pursuant to Article
80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation, and the relevance of those proceedings vis-à-vis the appeal
brought by the Appellant before this Board of Appeal – namely appeal case A001/2022. In this regard the
Board of Appeal notes the following points.
51. Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation states:
Article 80
Conditions for entries in the Registers
Without prejudice to other provisions of the basic Regulation or of this Regulation, a request for
an entry or a deletion of an entry in the Registers may be made by any interested person. The
request shall be made in writing, accompanied by supporting documents.
52. Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation is housed within the Implementing Rules Regulation.
That Regulation, as stated in its title, establishes implementing rules for the application of Council
Regulation (EC) No 2100/94 as regards proceedings before the Community Plant Variety Office. It is
settled case law that an implementing regulation must, if possible, be given an interpretation consistent
with the basic regulation (See, for example, Case C-32/00P Commission v Boehringer Ingelheim
Vetmedica GmbH and Another, ECLI:EU:C:2002:119, [2002] ECR I-1917, paragraph 53 and the case-law
cited therein) and that interpretation of EU law should, in principle, not negate the rights of an individual
(see, for example, Case C-340/97 Nazli and Others v Stadt Nürnberg, ECLI:EU:C:2000:77, [2000] ECR I-
957, paragraph 35). It therefore follows that the rules and requirements set out in the Implementing
Rules Regulation should be interpreted consistently and in line with the rules and requirements set out in
the primary legislation – namely the Basic Regulation, and should not be interpreted in such a way as to
negate the rights afforded to parties pursuant to the Basic Regulation.
53. Moreover, Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation expressly states that that provision applies
“without prejudice to other provisions of the basic Regulation…”. Article 80 of the Implementing Rules
Regulation cannot, therefore, be regarded as altering the scope or application of the provisions set out in
the Basic Regulation as that would have the effect of upsetting the balance that the legislature had
intended to achieve when adopting the Implementing Rules Regulation. The EU legislature alone has the
competence to strike that balance (See the Opinion of the Advocate General of 2 April 2020, Case
C-264/19, Constantin Film Verleih GmbH v YouTube LLC, Google Inc., ECLI:EU:C:2020:261, paragraphs
57-58 and the case-law cited therein).
54. It follows from the above, that Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation therefore cannot be
interpreted as calling into question the legal rights afforded under the Basic Regulation, including the right
to bring appeals before this Board of Appeal. This would not be in line with the legislature’s intention –
as explicitly expressed in Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation itself. Moreover, to do so may
negate or prevent parties from exercising their rights to bring appeals before the CPVO Board of Appeal
7

– a right that is codified and set out in the Basic Regulation. As such, in those situations where proceedings
pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation have commenced, and where the CPVO has
decided to suspend/stay those proceedings – as is the situation here – neither the initiation of proceedings
pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation, nor the suspension/stay of those
proceedings, can serve, per se, to negate, prevent or preclude parties from exercising their legal right to
bring an appeal before this Board of Appeal in accordance with Chapter V provisions of the Basic
Regulation, and related law. It follows that the CPVO may take action in the context of the Article 80 of
the Implementing Rules Regulation, but that action cannot serve to negate or deny the Appellant’s right
to bring an appeal before this Board of Appeal, or to prevent this Board of Appeal from hearing the appeal
or taking relevant action in relation to it.
55. The Board of Appeal further notes, in this regard, that the appeal procedure provided for in Chapter V of
the Basic Regulation – and which concerns this Board of Appeal on the one hand, and the ability for
natural and legal persons to submit requests pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation
on the other – cannot be regarded as the same or as serving the same legal purpose. Article 80 of the
Implementing Rules Regulation affords relevant persons the ability to submit a request. In this case the
Appellant has submitted a request, pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation, for the
particulars in the Register concerning the Queen PVR to be changed so as to delete the name and details
of the currently listed rightsholder (namely SGI), and to insert the Appellant’s name and relevant details.
At the same time the Appellant has lodged an appeal before this Board of Appeal to annul the initial action
taken by the CPVO to change the particulars in the Register concerning the Queen PVR by deleting the
Appellant’s name and relevant details as the rightsholder, and inserting SGI’s name and relevant details.
It therefore follows that although the Appellant seeks to achieve a similar outcome via both proceedings,
legally the procedures pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation and pursuant to Article
67 of the Basic Regulation, are distinct and different and not the same. That is because, amongst other
things, by the Appellant’s request submitted pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation,
the Appellant is requesting the CPVO to take certain action in the future regarding the entry in the Register
concerning the Queen PVR. Whereas, in contrast, by lodging an appeal before this Board of Appeal, the
Appellant is seeking, amongst other things, the annulment of an act taken by the CPVO in the past. As,
amongst other things, these administrative procedures must be regarded as separate and distinct,
contrary to the submissions from the CPVO, the Board of Appeal finds action or inaction taken by the
CPVO pursuant Article 80 of the IRR cannot be regarded as having a direct and/or automatic bearing on
appeal procedures before this Board of Appeal.
56. As regards to whether the Board of Appeal should stay the appeal proceedings, in a similar way as the
CPVO has suspended the processing of the Appellant’s Article 80 IRR request, the Board of Appeal notes
that it is for the Board of Appeal, in its discretion, to decide whether or not to stay appeal proceedings.
Relevant parties may lodge requests and applications to the Board of Appeal for appeal proceedings to
be stayed. However, merely because a party has submitted a request pursuant to Article 80 of the IRR
and the CPVO has suspended the assessment of that request – does not affect a party’s right to bring
appeal cases before this Board of Appeal, or for this Board of Appeal to hear the appeal case. In any
event, the Board of Appeal notes that it has not received a request or application from the parties in this
appeal case to stay the appeal proceedings, and the Board of Appeal has not deemed it necessary, of its
own initiative, to stay the appeal proceedings.
57. The Board of Appeal also notes that, as the CPVO has correctly pointed out during the written proceedings
and at the oral hearing, the publication of information in the Gazette is not a mere procedural formality,
but instead plays an important role in EU PVR legal system, not least as it can set the date by which time
appeals must be lodged before this Board of Appeal or the courts. It therefore follows that, decisions
adopted by the CPVO should, in principle, be published in the Gazette before or at the same time, as the
acts or measures prescribed within them in accordance with the principle of good administration, legal
certainty and legitimate expectation. Where this is not the case, it may result in the initiation of different
CPVO procedures at the same time – including, as is the case here – appeal proceedings before this Board
of Appeal and requests submitted pursuant to Article 80 of the IRR. For the avoidance of doubt, the Board
of Appeal takes the view that parties must be afforded their legal right to bring appeals before this Board
of Appeal which have suspensory effect pursuant to Article 67(3) of the Basic Regulation – and acts or
omissions by the CPVO should not call this legal right into question.
58. The Board of Appeal therefore concludes that merely because proceedings pursuant to Article 80 of the
Implementing Rules Regulation have been initiated and/or suspended/stayed, that cannot and does not
affect a party’s legal rights to bring an appeal case before this Board of Appeal. As such the Board of
Appeal concludes that proceedings initiated or suspended pursuant to Article 80 of the Implementing
8

Rules Regulation cannot and do not, per se, render proceedings before this Board of Appeal as
inadmissible, and, in any event, does not render appeal case A001/2022 as inadmissible.
59. Having addressed the preliminary issue of admissibility and proceedings initiated or suspended pursuant
to Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation, it is necessary for the Board of Appeal to address
whether or not the conditions of admissibility, as laid down in the Basic Regulation, the Implementing
Rules Regulation, and other relevant law - are met in this case.
60. As regards to the general subject matter and admissibility, the Board of Appeal notes the following points.
61. This appeal case concerns, amongst other things, the deletion and entering of information on the Register
concerning the Queen PVR, in particular the name and related details of the rightsholder.
62. In this regard, Article 67 of the Basic Regulation states, amongst other things, that “An appeal shall lie
from decisions of the Office which have been taken pursuant to […], as well as on decisions related to
[…] the entering or deletion of information in the Register pursuant to Article 87…” (Article 67(1) of the
Basic Regulation).
63. Article 87 of the Basic Regulation states, amongst other things, that “The Office shall keep a Register of
Community Plant Variety Rights wherein, after grant of a Community plant variety right, the following
particulars shall be entered: […] (d) the name and address of the holder, of the breeder and of any
procedural representative concerned” (Article 87(2) of the Basic Regulation).
64. It is not contested by the parties in this appeal case that the CPVO has, in fact and in law, adopted
measures to delete some of the details contained in the Register concerning the particulars for the Queen
PVR and entered other information in the Register concerning the particulars for the Queen PVR. More
specifically, it is not contested by the parties that the CPVO has adopted measures to delete the name
and relevant details of the Appellant as the rightsholder and enter SGI’s details as the rightsholder for the
Queen PVR. As expressly stated in Article 67 of the Basic Regulation, an appeal lies before this Board of
Appeal regarding the entering and deletion of certain titleholder information in the Register.
65. Given the above, the Board of Appeal concludes that the general subject matter falls within the scope of
measures which are subject to appeal before the CPVO Board of Appeal – namely, and amongst other
things, the deletion and entering of information on the Register concerning the Queen PVR.
66. As regards to the specific acts and measures being challenged by the Appellant, and whether or not those
acts are subject to appeal before the CPVO Board of Appeal, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO
essentially contests that although the Appellant has raised an appeal against the 14 December 2021 Act,
that act, according to the CPVO, is not subject to appeal for two reasons. Firstly, according to the CPVO,
the CPVO never intended that the 14 December 2021 Act to be appealable before the CPVO Board of
Appeal and, instead intended the 14 December 2021 Act merely to be a communication relating to the
proceedings initiated under Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation. Secondly, according to the
CPVO, the 14 December 2021 Act is not, and was not intended to be, according to the CPVO, a final act
but merely a communication informing the Appellant of the suspension/stay of a procedure (namely the
suspension/stay of the procedure under Article 80 of the Implementing Rules Regulation) which has not
been concluded or finalised.
67. Before assessing the merits of these arguments, the Board of Appeal recalls, as a preliminary matter, the
following points.
68. It is settled case-law that whether an act adopted by an EU institution or agency is open to appeal or
challenge, depends upon whether that act or measure, whatever its form, is intended to have binding
legal effects. If so, that act or measure is, in principle, open to appeal or challenge (see, for example,
Judgment of the General Court of 8 May 2018, Esso Raffinage, v European Chemicals Agency (ECHA),
Case T–283/15, ECLI:EU:T:2018:263, paragraphs 49; judgments of 31 March
1971, Commission v Council, 22/70, EU:C:1971:32, paragraph 42; of 2 March
1994, Parliament v Council, C-316/91, EU:C:1994:76, paragraph 8; and of 13 October 2011, Deutsche
Post and Germany v Commission, C-463/10 P and C-475/10 P, EU:C:2011:656, paragraph 36). If, on the
other hand, an act or measure does not produce binding legal effects, such as preparatory acts,
confirmatory measures and implementing measures, mere recommendations and opinions and, in
principle, internal instructions – then those acts and measures generally fall outside the scope of review
by the EU Courts and Boards of Appeal (see, for example, Judgment of the General Court of 8 May 2018,
Esso Raffinage, v European Chemicals Agency (ECHA), Case T–283/15, ECLI:EU:T:2018:263, paragraphs
9

50, order of 14 May 2012, Sepracor Pharmaceuticals (Ireland) v Commission, C-477/11 P, not published,
EU:C:2012:292, paragraph 52; see also, to that effect, the judgment of 12 September 2006, Reynolds
Tobacco and Others v Commission, C-131/03 P, EU:C:2006:541, paragraph 55 and the case-law cited).
The Board of Appeal further notes that Article 67 of the Basic Regulation provides that: “An appeal against
a decision which does not terminate proceedings as regards one of the parties may only be made in
conjunction with an appeal against the final decision, unless the decision provides for separate appeal”
(Article 67(4) of the Basic Regulation).
69. According to settled case-law:
The capacity of a measure to produce binding legal effects and, therefore, be open to challenge
[…] must be assessed in accordance with objective criteria, such as the contents of that
measure, taking into account, as appropriate, the context in which it was adopted and the powers
of the institution which adopted the measure (see, judgment of 13 February
2014, Hungary v Commission, C-31/13 P, EU:C:2014:70, paragraph 55 and the case-law cited).
The assessment of the content of a contested act consists in examining its substance (judgment
of 11 November 1981, IBM v Commission, 60/81, EU:C:1981:264, paragraph 9), whilst also
taking into account its wording (see, to that effect, the judgment of 20 March
1997, France v Commission, C-57/95, EU:C:1997:164, paragraphs 9 to 23). It is possible to
have regard also to subjective criteria, such as the intention of the author of the act in question
(see, to that effect, the judgments of 17 July 2008, Athinaïki Techniki v Commission, C-521/06 P,
EU:C:2008:422, paragraph 42, and of 26 January 2010, Internationaler
Hilfsfonds v Commission, C-362/08 P, EU:C:2010:40, paragraph 52). (see, for example,
Judgment of the General Court of 8 May 2018, Esso Raffinage, v European Chemicals Agency
(ECHA), Case T–283/15, ECLI:EU:T:2018:263, paragraphs 51).
70. In this case, the 14 December 2021 Act itself states amongst other things that
“On 29 September 2021, the Office registered the transfer of the Community plant variety right
for the variety ‘Queen’ of the species Citrus reticulata Blanco, registered under file No 2006/0947,
from Ángel Teresa Hermanos S.A. to Seedless Gold International S.L.” (emphasis added)
71. As stated at paragraphs [7-8] above, on the 29 September 2021, the CPVO updated the CPVO Register,
in particular the CPVO updated some of the particulars relating to the Queen PVR on the Register so as
to indicate that SGI was the rightsholder for the Queen PVR, not the Appellant.
72. The Board of Appeal therefore notes that on 29 September 2021 the CPVO did not adopt a mere initial
opinion or provisional view as regards the deletion and entering of details on the Register concerning the
Queen PVR. Instead, on 29 September 2021 the CPVO adopted a decision to delete and enter details on
the Register. Moreover, having adopted that decision, on 29 September 2021, the CPVO actually did, in
practice, take the relevant action - and deleted and entered the relevant details on the Register. It
therefore follows that the CPVO’s decision of 29 September 2021 was intended to be final and have
binding legal effects – and did have such effects in fact and law.
73. Moreover, it is clear from the CPVO’s submissions in this appeal case, that the CPVO does not regard the
deletion of the Appellant’s name and details from the Register as regards the Queen PVR, or the entering
of SGI’s name and details in the Register as regards the Queen PVR, as being a mere opinion or provisional
view. On the contrary, as the CPVO pointed out in its oral submissions during the oral hearing, and as
reflected in its comments to the Spanish Court as outlined at paragraph [26] above – in the CPVO’s view
– it is not possible to suspend the act concerning the change of the Register to indicate SGI as the
titleholder for the Queen PVR, as that act has already occurred. According to the CPVO, the only question
remaining is whether the particulars of the Queen PVR should, in the future, be changed again so as to
delete SGI’s details and insert those with the Appellants. It therefore follows that the CPVO’s act and
measure of resolving to amend the particulars of the Queen PVR so as to delete the Appellant’s name and
details, and replace those with SGI’s – must be regarded as a final act.
74. As regards to the CPVO’s contention that the CPVO did not intend for the 14 December 2021 Act to be
an act or measure open to challenge, the Board of Appeal note that although the subjective intention of
an institution or agency is of relevance in assessing whether, in fact and law, the act itself is open to
challenge – subjective intention is not the sole or exclusive factor that must be taken into account in
assessing whether the act or measure in question is open to challenge. Instead, regard must be had to,
10

amongst other things, the objective factors (see, for example, Judgment of 21 January 2021, Federal
Republic of Germany v Esso Raffinage, Case C-471/18 P, ECLI:EU:C:2021:48, paragraph 65 – and the
caselaw cited therein).
75. In this regard, the Board of Appeal notes that, in this case, as expressly and explicitly stated in the 14
December 2021 Act itself “On 29 September 2021, the Office registered the transfer”. The Board of Appeal
notes that the act of ‘registering a transfer’ involves, by its very nature, the deletion of certain information
in the particulars of the Queen PVR on the Register, and the entering of other information in those
particulars on the Register. i.e. the act of registering a transfer by its very nature constitutes a decision
to delete certain information, and enter other information, in the particulars of a PVR in the Register. This
is the case in particular where the deletion and/or entering of information on the Register cannot be
regarded as merely an editorial change, or an amendment of a minor or self-evident mistake via a
corrigenda or other similar act.
76. The Board of Appeal further notes that acts or measures concerning the deletion of information contained
in the particulars for a PVR on the Register legally constitute, as expressly stated in Article 67 of the Basic
Regulation, “decisions” by the CPVO, which are subject to appeal before this Board of Appeal. As such,
having regard to the legal context within which the measure was adopted in this case, more specifically
the CPVO’s act of determining to “register the transfer”, the Board of Appeal further finds that, according
to the relevant objective criteria, that the CPVO’s decision to delete and enter certain information on the
Register produced binding legal effects and is, therefore, an act subject to challenge.
77. It follows from the above, that the CPVO’s decision to delete and enter certain information from the
particulars on the Register concerning the Queen PVR, is an act open to appeal before this Board of
Appeal and, as such, is admissible in this regard.
78. Having assessed the relevant conditions of admissibility concerning the general subject matter and the
specific acts being contested, it is necessary for the Board of Appeal to determine whether the Appellant
is a party entitled to bring an appeal before this Board of Appeal. In this regard, the Board of Appeal
notes that, pursuant to Article 68 of the Basic Regulation, only those natural or legal persons to whom a
decision is addressed, or who are directly and individually concerned by it, are entitled to bring appeals
before this Board of Appeal. In interpreting the concept of ‘direct and individual concern’ in Article 68 of
the Basic Regulation, the Board of Appeal must be guided by the settled case law of the Court of Justice
of the European Union.
79. For a measure to be subject to an appeal or action for annulment, it is settled case-law that the measure
does not have to be formally entitled a decision – but must, instead, change the rights of the person to
whom it is addressed (see, Joined Cases 8-11/66 Cimenteries CBR Cementbedrijven NV v EEC Commission
[1967] ECR pages 75-91 [1967] CMLR, paragraph 103).
80. As the Board of Appeal has already stated at paragraphs [67-77] above, by resolving to register the
transfer of the Queen PVR right from the Appellant to SGI and thereby deleting and entering the relevant
details in the particulars stated on the Register for the Queen PVR, the CPVO adopted a decision. That
decision was first formally communicated by the CPVO to the Appellant on 14 December 2021 by way of
a letter. That letter was sent by the CPVO to SGI and, in addition, the Appellant. This is indicated in the
letter itself which states, amongst other things, “Sent by registered mail and email to: Angel Teresa
Hermanos S.A. (CPVO Client No 05449) refresh. NG/20210151029” and which contains the full name and
address of the Appellant within that document. It therefore follows that the 14 December 2021 Act itself
was addressed to the Appellant. It also follows that the decision taken by the CPVO on 29 September
2021 and concerning the deletion and entering of certain information on the Register concerning the
Queen PVR – and which the Appellant was informed of in the 14 December letter – was also addressed
to the Appellant. It follows that the Appellant is an addressee of the decision as provided for in Article 68
of the Basic Regulation.
81. In any event, the Board of Appeal notes that, pursuant to Article 68 of the Basic Regulation, natural or
legal persons who are not addressees, but who are directly and individually concerned by a decision, are
also entitled to bring appeals before this Board of Appeal.
82. In this regard, and as concerns direct concern, it is settled case law that for an act to be of direct concern
to a natural or legal person it must directly affect the legal situation of an appellant and leave no discretion
to the authorities responsible for implementing that act, such implementation being purely automatic and
resulting from EU law alone, without the application of other intermediate rules (See Judgment of the
11

European Court of Justice, 22 June 2021, Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela v Council of the European
Union, Case C-872/19 P, ECLI:EU:C:2021:507, paragraph 61 and the case-law cited therein).
83. In this case, the decision on 29 September 2021 to delete and enter information on the Register
concerning the Queen PVR to indicate the change of the rightsholder, the subsequent communication and
publication of that decision, and the change to the Register itself, impeded the Appellant’s legal ability to
assert itself as the legitimate legal owner of the PVR, and claim the legal, financial and other benefits of
possession and ownership of the PVR itself – including, for example, the collection of royalties. Moreover,
and in addition, the communication and publication of that decision, in principle set the time limit for
certain persons to bring appeals before this Board of Appeal, as set out in Article 69 of the Basic
Regulation. It therefore follows that the decision on 29 September 2021, and the subsequent
communication and publication of that decision and the change to the Register itself, directly affected the
legal situation of the Appellant.
84. Given the above, the Board of Appeal concludes that the decision to update the Register on 29 September
2021, and the subsequent communication and publication of that decision and the change to the Register
itself, is of direct concern to the Appellant.
85. As concerns individual concern, it is settled case law that persons other than those to whom a decision is
addressed may only claim to be individually concerned if that decision affects them by reason of certain
attributes which are peculiar to them or by reason of circumstances in which they are differentiated from
all other persons and by virtue of these factors distinguishes them individually just as in the case of the
person addressed. See Judgment of the European Court of Justice, 14 July 2022, Italian Republic, Comune
di Milano v Council of the European Union, European Parliament, Joined Cases C-106/19 and C-232/19,
ECLI:EU:C:2022:568, paragraph 68 and the caselaw cited therein).
86. In this case there are certain attributes which are peculiar to the Appellant and/or the circumstances,
which mean the Appellant is differentiated from all other persons. Specifically, it was the Appellant whose
name and address were deleted from the Register as regards the Queen PVR and whose particulars, in
this regard, were replaced by those of SGI. Moreover, it is by virtue of these factors that distinguishes
the Appellant individually in this case. Specifically, by virtue of the decision of 29 September 2021 to
update the Register, the Appellant alone, lost its listing as the holder of the right for the Queen PVR on
the Register and, as a result, its ability to assert legitimate ownership over the Queen PVR itself.
87. Given the above, the Board of Appeal concludes that the decision to update the particulars of the Queen
PVR on the Register on 29 September 2021 is of individual concern to the Appellant.
88. The Board of Appeal therefore finds that the Appellant as an addressee, and as a legal person with direct
and individual concern, is entitled to bring an appeal pursuant to Article 68 of the Basic Regulation.
89. As regards the time limit for the lodgement of an appeal, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO has
not contested that the Appellant has lodged its Notice of Appeal before the time limit set out in Article 69
of the Basic Regulation. Article 69 of the Basic Regulation provides that a Notice of Appeal must be lodged
at the Office “…within two months of the service of the decision where addressed to the appealing
person…”. The service of the decision in this case must be regarded as the first formal communication by
the CPVO to the Appellant informing the Appellant of the deletion and entry of information on the Register
concerning the Queen PVR and relating to the rightsholder information – namely the letter sent by the
CPVO to the Appellant of 14 December 2021. As stated above at paragraph [28], the Appellant lodged
the Notice of Appeal on 11 February 2022. The Board of Appeal therefore concludes that the Appellant
lodged the Notice of Appeal before the relevant deadline.
90. The second part of Article 69 of the Basic Regulation provides that “[in the absence of the service of the
decision addressed to the person appealing], [the Notice of appeal shall be filed in writing at the Office]
within two months of the publication of the decision.” To the extent that this deadline could be regarded
as of relevance, the Board of Appeal notes that the date of the publication of Gazette concerning the
change in the listed rightsholder for the Queen PVR was 15 December 2021. As stated above at paragraph
[28], the Appellant lodged the Notice of Appeal on 11 February 2022. The Board of Appeal therefore
concludes that the Appellant lodged the Notice of Appeal before the relevant deadline.
91. Having regard to the above, the Board of Appeal concludes that the present appeal case meets the
relevant conditions concerning admissibility, and that the appeal is therefore admissible.
12

92. Before proceeding to assess the substantive issues in this case, the Board of Appeal turns its attention to
the document submitted by the Appellant in this case, following the service of the Summons and entitled
“Juzgado de Instruccion No 2 de Orihuela NIG: 03099-43-2-2021-0008258”. The CPVO, together with the
Intervener, take the view that this document is inadmissible and cannot be included in the casefile or
considered by the Board of Appeal in its examination and deliberations of this case. That is because,
according to the CPVO: (1) the Appellant has lodged this document late, and (2) it does not contain
information of relevance to these proceedings as: it merely indicates that Spanish national legal
proceedings are continuing, and it cannot be used to imply or indicate that there has been any theft or
other non bona fide activity. The Appellant contests the arguments advanced by the CPVO and asserts
the document is admissible and must be considered.
93. The Board of Appeal notes that, at paragraph 27 of the summons to the oral hearing, it is stated that
“…no new evidence will be taken into consideration at the hearing unless the Board of Appeal decides
that the delay in offering the evidence is duly justified”. In this regard, the Board of Appeal notes that the
document became available to the Appellant a few weeks before the Appellant lodged the document with
the Board of Appeal. The Board of Appeal also notes that it is of benefit to this Board of Appeal to be kept
apprised of the developments in the legal proceedings before the Spanish Courts. Moreover, any delay in
lodging this document before the Board of Appeal should not be regarded as excessive given, amongst
other things, the multiple administrative and legal proceedings the Appellant is now engaged in, including,
for example: the appeal proceedings before this Board of Appeal, the Article 80 IRR procedure, and the
legal proceedings before the national Courts in Spain. As such, and without making any comment on the
relevance of the content or information contained with the document itself, the Board of Appeal concludes
this document is admissible, and should be included in the casefile and considered.
V. SUBSTANCE
94. In support of its appeal, the Appellant relies on a number of pleas and arguments, including the following:
(1) The CPVO did not properly consider the information and evidence before registering the transfer
of the Queen PVR on the Register.
(2) The CPVO did not give the Appellant sufficient warning of the registration of the transfer.
(3) The CPVO failed to stop, suspend or delay the decision to register the transfer.
(4) The CPVO failed to sufficiently state the reasons for the decision to register the transfer.
95. As a preliminary issue, and before assessing the merits of the pleas and arguments put forward by the
Parties, the Board of Appeal recalls, that the Board of Appeal is strictly limited and restricted in the scope
of its review. More specifically, the Board of Appeal is limited and restricted to assessing certain action
and inaction taken by the CPVO in specific fields. The Board of Appeal has no legal or other powers to
review issues outside of its competencies. Issues such as alleged theft, alleged deceitful or fraudulent
activities, and/or other related alleged non bona fide and criminal activities on the part of natural or legal
persons, are generally outside the scope of the CPVO Board of Appeal competencies, and therefore not
issues the CPVO Board of Appeal can or will opine. As such, the Board of Appeal shall, in these appeal
proceedings, strictly adhere to the scope and remit of its powers, and assess only those issues within its
mandate to review which, in this appeal case, primarily concern action and inaction taken by the CPVO
as regards the registration of the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant company to SGI. The
CPVO Board of Appeal remains cognizant of the fact that whilst the appeal proceedings before the CPVO
Board of Appeal have been ongoing in this case, there are legal proceedings also ongoing before national
Courts in one of the EU Member States. For the avoidance of doubt, the assessments and findings of the
CPVO Board of Appeal in this appeal case have not been impacted, and do not seek to impact or affect,
in any way whatsoever, the assessments and findings by other EU or national Courts, or other bodies.
First plea alleging the CPVO did not properly consider the relevant information and evidence
96. By this plea the Appellant essentially alleges that: (1) The CPVO failed to comply with the duty of care,
and carry out a careful and diligent examination of all the facts and circumstances, before adopting the
decision to change the particulars on the Register concerning the PVR and/or the decision to transfer the
register; and (2) The CPVO failed to take into consideration all the information that the CPVO was required
to consider before adopting the decision to change the particulars on the Register concerning the PVR
and/or the decision to register the transfer.
97. The CPVO, together with the Intervener, rejects this plea and the related lines of argument as unfounded.
According to the CPVO, the onus is on titleholders to keep the CPVO up-to-date on the individuals
nominated to act on their behalf. Where a titleholder is a legal entity which has appointed a representative,
and where that individual no longer has the legal authority to represent or act on behalf of the legal entity
13

in question, it is, according to the CPVO, incumbent on the legal entity in question to immediately inform
the CPVO of that fact. According to the CPVO, there is a ‘presumption of validity’ meaning that the CPVO
should be able to presume that all persons with whom it interacts with – act in good faith. The CPVO
points out that it has a large number of files and that reliance on the ‘presumption of good faith’ is an
important element in ensuring the CPVO is able to manage its workload.
Findings of the Board of Appeal
98. As a preliminary issue, and before assessing the merits of the plea and arguments put forward by the
Parties, the Board of Appeal recalls the general legal framework and guidance.
99. Firstly, the Board of Appeal recalls the general legal framework and guidance concerning the registration
of a transfer of a PVR which includes, but is not limited, to the following.
100. Article 23 of the Basic Regulation states:
Article 23
Transfer
1. A Community plant variety right may be the object of a transfer to one or more successors in title.
2. Transfer of a Community plant variety right by assignment can be made only to successors who comply
with the conditions laid down in Article 12 and 82. It shall be made in writing and shall require the signature
of the parties to the contract, except when it is a result of a judgement or of any other acts terminating
court proceedings. Otherwise it shall be void.
3. Save as otherwise provided in Article 100, a transfer shall have no bearing on the rights acquired by
third parties before the date of transfer.
4. A transfer shall not take effect for the Office and may not be cited vis-à-vis third parties unless
documentary evidence thereof as provided for in the implementing rules is provided and until it has been
entered in the Register of Community Plant Variety Rights. A transfer that has not yet been entered in the
Register may, however, be cited vis-à-vis third parties who have acquired rights after the date of transfer
but who knew of the transfer at the date on which they acquired those rights.”
101. Article 79 of the IRR states:
Article 79
Entry of transfer of a Community plant variety right
1. Any transfer of Community plant variety rights shall be entered in the Register of Community Plant
Variety Rights on production of documentary evidence of the transfer, or of official documents confirming
the transfer, or of such extracts from those documents as suffice to establish the transfer. The Office shall
retain a copy of those pieces of documentary evidence in its files.
The President of the Office shall determine the form in and the conditions under which those pieces of
documentary evidence are to be retained in the files of the Office.
2. The entry of a transfer may be refused only in the event of failure to comply with the conditions laid
down in paragraph 1 and in Article 23 of the basic Regulation.
3. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall apply to any transfer of an entitlement to a Community plant variety right for
which an application has been entered in the Register of Applications for Community Plant Variety Rights.
The reference to the Register of Community Plant Variety Rights shall be understood as a reference to
the Register of Applications for Community Plant Variety Rights.
102. CPVO Guidance, as set out on the CPVO website under “FAQs” or Frequently Asked Questions, states:1
 How to register a transfer of CPVR ownership?
A deed of assignment whereby the present holder (assignor) transfers the concerned CPVRs or/and
application(s) to the new holder (assignee) must be provided. It must be duly dated and signed by both
holders or parties. A template assignment is available on our website at:
http://cpvo.europa.eu/en/applications-and-examinations/filing-application/filing-paper. Please note that
there is a fee of € 100 for the transfer recordal and a publication in the Official Gazette of the Office.
103. The CPVO document entitled “Assignments - Validity”2 states, amongst other things, that: “Please note
that: 1. There is no official assignment form. 2. In view of its laissez-faire approach, the Office respects
the contractual will of the parties involved. 3. The Office does not encourage anyone to renounce to their
rights” and that “The deed of assignment required consists of any valid act of disposal of a right by its
holder, as, for instance, a contract, a declaration of assignment, a letter, a will or any other document that
serves the purpose of transfer of a right from its rightful owner to another party.” That document also sets
1 https://cpvo.europa.eu/en/help-center/faq
2 https://cpvo.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/assignment_note_en_0.pdf
14

out the conditions that need to be satisfied to ensure the “deed of assignment” is considered valid by the
CPVO. Those include:
1. The names of the assignor (original owner) and of the assignee (new owner). If dealing with a
legal person (e.g. companies) do not forget to mention the name of the natural person who has
the right to legally represent the company. Said information enables the Office to assess the
validity of the signature(s) present on the document filed.
2. The breeder's reference or the denomination of the plant variety.
3. The botanical taxon of the plant variety.
4. The territory concerned, namely where the title is valid (European Union).
5. A clearly worded transfer of the entitlement to apply for Plant Breeders’ rights or of the
application for registration or of the registered Plant Breeders’ right, if it was already granted.
Please, bear in mind that a mere licence is not a valid assignment for our purposes, as it provides
a permission to benefit from a certain right without any transfer of ownership.
6. The deed of assignment shall be dated.
7. If the breeder is an employee, the entitlement to the Community plant variety right shall be
determined in accordance with the national law applicable to the employment relationship in the
context of which the variety was bred, or discovered and developed, as indicated under Article
11(4) of Council Regulation 2100/94. To that effect, the relevant national law applicable should
be precisely cited.
8. A digital copy of the relevant document is sufficient. However, the original or a certified copy
of the deed will be required in case of doubt.
9. The assignment must also contain the signatures of both parties if it is made during the
application procedure or after a title has been granted (Articles 23 and 26 Council Regulation
2100/94). If the assignment is carried out before an application for a Plant Variety right is filed to
the CPVO, the obligation of signature should be assessed pursuant to the national law applicable
to the assignment. It is, in any case, advisable that both parties sign the assignment.
104. Although not strictly forming part of the legal framework and guidance relating to PVRs per se, the Board
of Appeal does take note of the EUIPO Trademark and Design guidelines, including the current EUIPO
guidelines,3 and EUIPO guidelines in place at the time the CPVO registered the transfer of the Queen PVR
in this case. As regards the latter, the Board of Appeal notes that certain parts of the EUIPO Trade Mark
Guidelines4 clearly reflected the importance of the duty of diligence (see paragraph [111] below), and set
out ways to best ensure it is discharged in practice, stating, for example: “A transfer may be registered
only when it is proven by documents duly establishing the transfer […]” and that “If the Office has reason
to doubt the accuracy or veracity of the document, it may require additional proof”.
105. Secondly, the Board of Appeal recalls the general legal framework and guidance concerning the
appointment of representatives which includes, but is not limited, to the following.
106. Article 82 of the Basic Regulation states: “Persons who are not domiciled or do not have a seat or an
establishment within the territory of the Community may participate as party to proceedings before the
Office only if they have designated a procedural representative who is domiciled or has his seat or an
establishment within the territory of the Community.” Articles 73-74, and 66a of the IRR, amongst others,
regulate the affairs of procedural representatives including their credentials, designation and notifications
to procedural representatives.
107. Article 2(4) of the IRR states: “4. Where a party to proceedings is a legal person, it shall also be
designated by the name and address of the natural person legally representing the party to proceedings
by virtue of the relevant national legislation. The provisions of paragraph 2 shall apply mutatis mutandis
to such natural person. The Office may permit derogations from the provisions of the first sentence of the
first subparagraph.”

108. Article 73 of the IRR states:
Article 73
Designation of a procedural representative
1. Any designation of a procedural representative shall be communicated to the Office. The
communication shall contain the name and address of the procedural representative; Article 2(2) and (3)
shall apply mutatis mutandis.
2. Without prejudice to Article 2(4), the communication referred to in paragraph 1 shall also identify as
such any employee of the party to proceedings. An employee may not be designated as a procedural
representative within the meaning of Article 82 of the basic Regulation.
3. Failure to comply with the provisions of paragraphs 1 and 2 shall lead to the communication being
deemed not to have been received.
4. A procedural representative whose mandate has ended shall continue to be considered as procedural
representative until the termination of his mandate has been communicated to the Office. Subject to any
provisions to the contrary contained therein, a mandate shall however, terminate vis-à-vis the Office upon
the death of the person who conferred it.
109. The CPVO Guidance ‘Notes for Applicants’ and FAQs5 states, amongst other things: “[…] Applicants from
outside the EU must appoint a representative domiciled or seated or established within the territory of the
European Union. The procedural representative form is available on the website.
The procedural representative
• Applicant based outside the EU
If you do not have a domicile, legal seat or establishment within the European Union, you are required to
appoint a procedural representative residing within the European Union in order to receive any
communication from the Office. Under these circumstances, the procedural representative may not be
your employee. A form for this purpose is available on the CPVO’s website.
• Applicant based within the EU
If you have a domicile, legal seat or establishment in the European Union, you are not obliged to appoint
a procedural representative but you may do so if you wish. Under these circumstances, the procedural
representative may be your employee. You can make use of the form available on the CPVO website.
The legal representative
Where the applicant is a legal person (e.g. a company) rather than a natural person (an individual), the
Office must be informed of the name and address of the natural person who is empowered under the
applicable national legislation to represent the legal person.”
110. CPVO Guidance ‘Notes on the completion of the application form’6 states, amongst other things:
“POINT 02 - PROCEDURAL REPRESENTATIVE
Where a procedural representative is mandatory (applicants not having a domicile, seat or establishment
within the territory of the European Union), the procedural representative may not be an employee of the
applicant. If the appointment of a procedural representative is not mandatory in your case, you may
nevertheless appoint one. The person appointed may not be an employee. You may make use of the
Office’s form “Designation of a Procedural Representative”. Please note that this form needs to be signed
by the person duly authorized by the applicant.”
111. In addition to the general legal framework and guidance, some of which is set out above, the Board of
Appeal recalls the relevant jurisprudence. More specifically, that it is settled case-law that, when the CPVO
or other relevant EU agency assess whether to register the transfer of a PVR or similar IP right, the CPVO
is required to examine carefully and impartially all the relevant factual and legal aspects of the individual
case (see, General Court Judgment of 22 September 2021, Marina Yachting Brand Management Co. Ltd
v EUIPO, Case T-169/20, ECLI:EU:T:2021:609, hereinafter “Marina Yachting”, paragraph 58 and the case-
law cited therein). More specifically, it is settled case-law that the CPVO must, in these circumstances, act
in accordance with the duty of diligence (see, by analogy, Marina Yachting, paragraphs 69, 74, 58 and
the paragraphs referred to therein). Pursuant to that duty, it is incumbent upon the CPVO to amongst
other things “[…] diligently take into account facts that are capable of having legal implications for the
entries it makes in that register” (see Case T-169/20, paragraph 58).

center/faq
